book: Class 12 - Mathematics - Exemplar Book by NCERT
question_text: Find the position vector of a point A in space such that {tex}\overrightarrow {OA} {/tex} is inclined at {tex}6{0^0}{/tex} to OX and at {tex}{45^0}{/tex} to OY and {tex}\left| {\overrightarrow {OA} } \right| = 10{/tex} units.
question_html: Find the position vector of a point A in space such that {tex}\overrightarrow {OA} {/tex} is inclined at {tex}6{0^0}{/tex} to OX and at {tex}{45^0}{/tex} to OY and {tex}\left| {\overrightarrow {OA} } \right| = 10{/tex} units.
solution_text: Since, {tex}{\overrightarrow {OA} }{/tex} is inclined at {tex}{60^0}{/tex} to OX and at {tex}{45^0}{/tex} to OY. Let {tex}{\overrightarrow {OA} }{/tex} make angle {tex}\alpha {/tex} with OZ. {tex}\therefore {\cos ^2}{60^0} + {\cos ^2}{45^0} + {\cos ^2}\alpha = 1{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow {\left( {\frac{1}{2}} \right)^2} + {\left( {\frac{1}{{\sqrt 2 }}} \right)^2} + {\cos ^2}\alpha = 1{/tex} {tex}\left[ {\because {l^2} + {m^2} + {n^2} = 1} \right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{1}{4} + \frac{1}{2}+{\cos ^2}\alpha = 1{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow {\cos ^2}\alpha = 1 - \left( {\frac{1}{2} + \frac{1}{4}} \right){/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow {\cos ^2}\alpha = 1 - \left( {\frac{6}{8}} \right){/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow {\cos ^2}\alpha = \frac{1}{4}{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \cos \alpha = \frac{1}{2} = \cos {60^0}{/tex} {tex}\therefore \alpha = {60^0}{/tex} {tex}\therefore \overrightarrow {OA} = \overrightarrow {|OA|} \left( {\frac{1}{2}\hat i + \frac{1}{{\sqrt 2 }}\hat j + \frac{1}{2}\hat k} \right){/tex} {tex}= 10\left( {\frac{1}{2}\hat i + \frac{1}{{\sqrt 2 }}\hat j + \frac{1}{2}\hat k} \right)\,\,\left[ {\because \left| {\overrightarrow {OA} } \right| = 10} \right]{/tex} {tex} = 5\hat i + 5\sqrt 2 \hat j + 5\hat k{/tex}
solution_html:
Since, {tex}{\overrightarrow {OA} }{/tex} is inclined at {tex}{60^0}{/tex} to OX and at {tex}{45^0}{/tex} to OY. Let {tex}{\overrightarrow {OA} }{/tex} make angle {tex}\alpha {/tex} with OZ.

{tex}\therefore {\cos ^2}{60^0} + {\cos ^2}{45^0} + {\cos ^2}\alpha = 1{/tex}

{tex}\Rightarrow {\left( {\frac{1}{2}} \right)^2} + {\left( {\frac{1}{{\sqrt 2 }}} \right)^2} + {\cos ^2}\alpha = 1{/tex} {tex}\left[ {\because {l^2} + {m^2} + {n^2} = 1} \right]{/tex}

{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{1}{4} + \frac{1}{2}+{\cos ^2}\alpha = 1{/tex}

{tex}\Rightarrow {\cos ^2}\alpha = 1 - \left( {\frac{1}{2} + \frac{1}{4}} \right){/tex}

{tex} \Rightarrow {\cos ^2}\alpha = 1 - \left( {\frac{6}{8}} \right){/tex}

{tex} \Rightarrow {\cos ^2}\alpha = \frac{1}{4}{/tex}

{tex} \Rightarrow \cos \alpha = \frac{1}{2} = \cos {60^0}{/tex}

{tex}\therefore \alpha = {60^0}{/tex}

{tex}\therefore \overrightarrow {OA} = \overrightarrow {|OA|} \left( {\frac{1}{2}\hat i + \frac{1}{{\sqrt 2 }}\hat j + \frac{1}{2}\hat k} \right){/tex}

{tex}= 10\left( {\frac{1}{2}\hat i + \frac{1}{{\sqrt 2 }}\hat j + \frac{1}{2}\hat k} \right)\,\,\left[ {\because \left| {\overrightarrow {OA} } \right| = 10} \right]{/tex}

{tex} = 5\hat i + 5\sqrt 2 \hat j + 5\hat k{/tex}

question_text: Find the vector equation of the line which is parallel to the vector {tex}3\hat i - 2\hat j + 6\hat k{/tex} and which passes through the point {tex}\left( {1, - 2,3} \right){/tex} .
question_html: Find the vector equation of the line which is parallel to the vector {tex}3\hat i - 2\hat j + 6\hat k{/tex} and which passes through the point {tex}\left( {1, - 2,3} \right){/tex}.
solution_text: Let {tex}\vec a = 3\hat i - 2\hat j + 6\hat k{/tex} and {tex}\vec b = \hat i - 2\hat j + 3\hat k{/tex} So, vector equation of the line, which is parallel to the vector {tex}\vec a = 3\hat i - 2\hat j + 6\hat k{/tex} and passes through the vector {tex}\vec b = \hat i - 2\hat j + 3\hat k{/tex} is {tex}\vec r = \vec b + \lambda \vec a{/tex} {tex}\therefore \vec r = \left( {\hat i - 2\hat j + 3\hat k} \right) + \lambda \left( {3\hat i - 2\hat j + 6\hat k} \right){/tex}
solution_html:
Let {tex}\vec a = 3\hat i - 2\hat j + 6\hat k{/tex} and {tex}\vec b = \hat i - 2\hat j + 3\hat k{/tex}
So, vector equation of the line, which is parallel to the vector {tex}\vec a = 3\hat i - 2\hat j + 6\hat k{/tex} and passes through the vector {tex}\vec b = \hat i - 2\hat j + 3\hat k{/tex} is {tex}\vec r = \vec b + \lambda \vec a{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \vec r = \left( {\hat i - 2\hat j + 3\hat k} \right) + \lambda \left( {3\hat i - 2\hat j + 6\hat k} \right){/tex}

question_text: Show that the lines {tex}\frac{x-1}{2}=\frac{y-2}{3}=\frac{z-3}{4}{/tex} and {tex}\frac{x-4}{5}=\frac{y-1}{2}=z{/tex} intersect. Also, find their point of intersection.
question_html: Show that the lines {tex}\frac{x-1}{2}=\frac{y-2}{3}=\frac{z-3}{4}{/tex} and {tex}\frac{x-4}{5}=\frac{y-1}{2}=z{/tex} intersect. Also, find their point of intersection.
solution_text: We have lines {tex}L_{1}: \frac{x-1}{2}=\frac{y-2}{3}=\frac{z-3}{4}=\lambda{/tex} and {tex}L_{2}: \frac{x-4}{5}=\frac{y-1}{2}=z=\mu{/tex} Any point on the line L 1 is {tex}(2 \lambda+1,3 \lambda+2,4 \lambda+3){/tex} Any point on the line L 2 is {tex}(5 \mu+4,2 \mu+1, \mu){/tex} If lines intersect then there exists a value of {tex}\lambda, \mu{/tex} for which {tex}(2 \lambda+1,3 \lambda+2,4 \lambda+3)=(5 \mu+4,2 \mu+1, \mu){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad 2 \lambda+1=5 \mu+4,3 \lambda+2=2 \mu+1{/tex} and {tex}4 \lambda+3=\mu{/tex} Solving first two equations we get {tex}\lambda=-1, \mu=-1{/tex} These values of {tex}\lambda=-1, \mu=-1{/tex} also satisfy the third equation. Thus lines intersect. Also the point of intersections is (-1, -1, -1).
solution_html:
We have lines
{tex}L_{1}: \frac{x-1}{2}=\frac{y-2}{3}=\frac{z-3}{4}=\lambda{/tex}
and {tex}L_{2}: \frac{x-4}{5}=\frac{y-1}{2}=z=\mu{/tex}
Any point on the line L1 is {tex}(2 \lambda+1,3 \lambda+2,4 \lambda+3){/tex}
Any point on the line L2 is {tex}(5 \mu+4,2 \mu+1, \mu){/tex}
If lines intersect then there exists a value of {tex}\lambda, \mu{/tex} for which {tex}(2 \lambda+1,3 \lambda+2,4 \lambda+3)=(5 \mu+4,2 \mu+1, \mu){/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad 2 \lambda+1=5 \mu+4,3 \lambda+2=2 \mu+1{/tex} and {tex}4 \lambda+3=\mu{/tex}
Solving first two equations we get {tex}\lambda=-1, \mu=-1{/tex}
These values of {tex}\lambda=-1, \mu=-1{/tex} also satisfy the third equation.
Thus lines intersect.
Also the point of intersections is (-1, -1, -1).

question_text: Find the angles between the lines {tex}\vec r = 3\hat i - 2\hat j + 6\hat k + \lambda \left( {2\hat i + \hat j + 2\hat k} \right){/tex} and {tex}\vec r = \left( {2\hat i - 5\hat k} \right) + \mu \left( {6\hat i + 3\hat j + 2\hat k} \right){/tex}
question_html: Find the angles between the lines {tex}\vec r = 3\hat i - 2\hat j + 6\hat k + \lambda \left( {2\hat i + \hat j + 2\hat k} \right){/tex}and {tex}\vec r = \left( {2\hat i - 5\hat k} \right) + \mu \left( {6\hat i + 3\hat j + 2\hat k} \right){/tex}
solution_text: We have, {tex}\vec r = 3\hat i - 2\hat j + 6\hat k + \lambda \left( {2\hat i + \hat j + 2\hat k} \right){/tex} And {tex}\vec r = \left( {2\hat i - 5\hat k} \right) + \mu \left( {6\hat i + 3\hat j + 2\hat k} \right){/tex} Where, {tex}{\vec a_1} = 3\hat i - 2\hat j + 6\hat k,{\vec b_1} = 2\hat i + \hat j + 2\hat k{/tex} And {tex}{\vec a_2} = 2\vec j - 5\vec k,{\vec b_2} = 6\hat i + 3\hat j + 2\hat k{/tex} If {tex}\theta {/tex} is angle between the lines, then {tex}\cos \theta = \frac{{\left| {{{\vec b}_1}.{{\vec b}_2}} \right|}}{{\left| {{{\vec b}_1}} \right|.\left| {{{\vec b}_1}} \right|}}{/tex} {tex}= \frac{{\left| {\left( {2\hat i + \hat j + 2\hat k} \right).\left( {6\hat i + 3\hat j + 2\hat k} \right)} \right|}}{{\left| {2\hat i + \hat j + 2\hat k} \right|\left| {6\hat i + 3\hat j + 2\hat k} \right|}}{/tex} {tex}= \frac{{\left| {12 + 3 + 4} \right|}}{{\sqrt 9 \sqrt {49} }} = \frac{{19}}{{21}}{/tex} {tex}\theta = {\cos ^{ - 1}}\frac{{19}}{{21}}{/tex}
solution_html:
We have, {tex}\vec r = 3\hat i - 2\hat j + 6\hat k + \lambda \left( {2\hat i + \hat j + 2\hat k} \right){/tex}

And {tex}\vec r = \left( {2\hat i - 5\hat k} \right) + \mu \left( {6\hat i + 3\hat j + 2\hat k} \right){/tex}

Where, {tex}{\vec a_1} = 3\hat i - 2\hat j + 6\hat k,{\vec b_1} = 2\hat i + \hat j + 2\hat k{/tex}

And {tex}{\vec a_2} = 2\vec j - 5\vec k,{\vec b_2} = 6\hat i + 3\hat j + 2\hat k{/tex}

If {tex}\theta {/tex} is angle between the lines, then

{tex}\cos \theta = \frac{{\left| {{{\vec b}_1}.{{\vec b}_2}} \right|}}{{\left| {{{\vec b}_1}} \right|.\left| {{{\vec b}_1}} \right|}}{/tex}

{tex}= \frac{{\left| {\left( {2\hat i + \hat j + 2\hat k} \right).\left( {6\hat i + 3\hat j + 2\hat k} \right)} \right|}}{{\left| {2\hat i + \hat j + 2\hat k} \right|\left| {6\hat i + 3\hat j + 2\hat k} \right|}}{/tex}

{tex}= \frac{{\left| {12 + 3 + 4} \right|}}{{\sqrt 9 \sqrt {49} }} = \frac{{19}}{{21}}{/tex}

{tex}\theta = {\cos ^{ - 1}}\frac{{19}}{{21}}{/tex}

question_text: Prove that the line through A (0, -1, -1) and B (4, 5,1) intersects the line through C (3,9,4) and D (-4, 4, 4).
question_html: Prove that the line through A (0, -1, -1) and B (4, 5,1) intersects the line through C (3,9,4) and D (-4, 4, 4).
solution_text: The equation of line through {tex} A (0,-1,-1)\ and\ B (4, 5,1){/tex} is {tex}\frac { x - 0 } { 4 - 0 } = \frac { y + 1 } { 5 + 1 } = \frac { z + 1 } { 1 + 1 }{/tex} i.e. {tex}\frac { x } { 4 } = \frac { y + 1 } { 6 } = \frac { z + 1 } { 2 }{/tex} .....(i) Equation of line through {tex}C (3,9,4)\ and\ D (-4,4,4){/tex} is {tex}\frac { x - 3 } { - 4 - 3 } = \frac { y - 9 } { 4 - 9 } = \frac { z - 4 } { 0 }{/tex} i.e., {tex}\frac { x - 3 } { - 7 } = \frac { y - 9 } { - 5 } = \frac { z - 4 } { 0 }{/tex} ....(ii) We know that, the lines {tex}\frac { x - x _ { 1 } } { a _ { 1 } } = \frac { y - y _ { 1 } } { b _ { 1 } } = \frac { z - z _ { 1 } } { c _ { 1 } }{/tex} and {tex}\frac { x - x _ { 2 } } { a _ { 2 } } = \frac { y - y _ { 2 } } { b _ { 2 } } = \frac { z - z _ { 2 } } { c _ { 2 } }{/tex} will intersect, if {tex}\left| \begin{array} { c c c } { x _ { 2 } - x _ { 1 } } & { y _ { 2 } - y _ { 1 } } & { z _ { 2 } - z _ { 1 } } \\ { a _ { 1 } } & { b _ { 1 } } & { c _ { 1 } } \\ { a _ { 2 } } & { b _ { 2 } } & { c _ { 2 } } \end{array} \right| = 0{/tex} :. The given lines will intersect, if {tex}\left| \begin{array} { c c c } { 3 - 0 } & { 9 - ( - 1 ) } & { 4 - ( - 1 ) } \\ { 4 } & { 6 } & { 2 } \\ { - 7 } & { - 5 } & { 0 } \end{array} \right| = 0{/tex} Now, {tex}\left| \begin{array} { c c c } { 3 - 0 } & { 9 - ( - 1 ) } & { 4 - ( - 1 ) } \\ { 4 } & { 6 } & { 2 } \\ { - 7 } & { - 5 } & { 0 } \end{array} \right| = \left| \begin{array} { c c c } { 3 } & { 10 } & { 5 } \\ { 4 } & { 6 } & { 2 } \\ { - 7 } & { - 5 } & { 0 } \end{array} \right|{/tex} {tex}= 3(0+ 10 -10 (0+ 14) + 5 (-20+ 42){/tex} {tex}= 30-140 + 110 =0{/tex} Hence, the given lines intersect.
solution_html:
The equation of line through{tex} A (0,-1,-1)\ and\ B (4, 5,1){/tex} is
{tex}\frac { x - 0 } { 4 - 0 } = \frac { y + 1 } { 5 + 1 } = \frac { z + 1 } { 1 + 1 }{/tex}
i.e. {tex}\frac { x } { 4 } = \frac { y + 1 } { 6 } = \frac { z + 1 } { 2 }{/tex}.....(i)
Equation of line through {tex}C (3,9,4)\ and\ D (-4,4,4){/tex} is
{tex}\frac { x - 3 } { - 4 - 3 } = \frac { y - 9 } { 4 - 9 } = \frac { z - 4 } { 0 }{/tex}
i.e., {tex}\frac { x - 3 } { - 7 } = \frac { y - 9 } { - 5 } = \frac { z - 4 } { 0 }{/tex} ....(ii)
We know that, the lines
{tex}\frac { x - x _ { 1 } } { a _ { 1 } } = \frac { y - y _ { 1 } } { b _ { 1 } } = \frac { z - z _ { 1 } } { c _ { 1 } }{/tex}and
{tex}\frac { x - x _ { 2 } } { a _ { 2 } } = \frac { y - y _ { 2 } } { b _ { 2 } } = \frac { z - z _ { 2 } } { c _ { 2 } }{/tex}will intersect,
if{tex}\left| \begin{array} { c c c } { x _ { 2 } - x _ { 1 } } & { y _ { 2 } - y _ { 1 } } & { z _ { 2 } - z _ { 1 } } \\ { a _ { 1 } } & { b _ { 1 } } & { c _ { 1 } } \\ { a _ { 2 } } & { b _ { 2 } } & { c _ { 2 } } \end{array} \right| = 0{/tex}
:. The given lines will intersect, if
{tex}\left| \begin{array} { c c c } { 3 - 0 } & { 9 - ( - 1 ) } & { 4 - ( - 1 ) } \\ { 4 } & { 6 } & { 2 } \\ { - 7 } & { - 5 } & { 0 } \end{array} \right| = 0{/tex}
Now,
{tex}\left| \begin{array} { c c c } { 3 - 0 } & { 9 - ( - 1 ) } & { 4 - ( - 1 ) } \\ { 4 } & { 6 } & { 2 } \\ { - 7 } & { - 5 } & { 0 } \end{array} \right| = \left| \begin{array} { c c c } { 3 } & { 10 } & { 5 } \\ { 4 } & { 6 } & { 2 } \\ { - 7 } & { - 5 } & { 0 } \end{array} \right|{/tex}
{tex}= 3(0+ 10 -10 (0+ 14) + 5 (-20+ 42){/tex}
{tex}= 30-140 + 110 =0{/tex}
Hence, the given lines intersect.

question_text: Prove that the lines {tex}x = py + q,z = ry + s{/tex} and {tex}x = p'y + q,z = r'y + s'{/tex} are perpendicular if {tex}pp' + rr' + 1 = 0{/tex} .
question_html: Prove that the lines {tex}x = py + q,z = ry + s{/tex} and {tex}x = p'y + q,z = r'y + s'{/tex}are perpendicular if {tex}pp' + rr' + 1 = 0{/tex}.
solution_text: We have, {tex}x = py + q \Rightarrow y = \frac{{x - q}}{p}{/tex} ...(i) And {tex}z = ry + s \Rightarrow y = \frac{{z - s}}{r}{/tex} ...(ii) {tex} \Rightarrow \frac{{x - q}}{p} = \frac{y}{1} = \frac{{z - s}}{r}{/tex} [Using Eqs. (i) and (ii)] ...(iii) Similarly, {tex}\frac{{x - q'}}{{p'}} = \frac{y}{1} = \frac{{z - s'}}{{r'}}{/tex} ...(iv) From Eqs. (iii) and (iv), {tex}{a_1} = p,{b_1} = 1,{c_1} = r{/tex} and {tex}{a_2} = p',{b_2} = 1,{c_2} = r'{/tex} if these given lines are perpendicular to each other, then {tex}{a_1}{a_2} + {b_1}{b_2} + {c_1}{c_2} = 0{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow pp' + 1 + rr' = 0{/tex} Which is the required condition.
solution_html:
We have, {tex}x = py + q \Rightarrow y = \frac{{x - q}}{p}{/tex}...(i)

And {tex}z = ry + s \Rightarrow y = \frac{{z - s}}{r}{/tex} ...(ii)

{tex} \Rightarrow \frac{{x - q}}{p} = \frac{y}{1} = \frac{{z - s}}{r}{/tex} [Using Eqs. (i) and (ii)] ...(iii)

Similarly, {tex}\frac{{x - q'}}{{p'}} = \frac{y}{1} = \frac{{z - s'}}{{r'}}{/tex} ...(iv)

From Eqs. (iii) and (iv),

{tex}{a_1} = p,{b_1} = 1,{c_1} = r{/tex}

and {tex}{a_2} = p',{b_2} = 1,{c_2} = r'{/tex}

if these given lines are perpendicular to each other, then

{tex}{a_1}{a_2} + {b_1}{b_2} + {c_1}{c_2} = 0{/tex}

{tex} \Rightarrow pp' + 1 + rr' = 0{/tex}

Which is the required condition.

question_text: Find the equation of a plane which bisects perpendicularly the line joining the points {tex}A\left( {2,3,4} \right){/tex} and {tex}B\left( {4,5,8} \right){/tex} at right angles.
question_html: Find the equation of a plane which bisects perpendicularly the line joining the points {tex}A\left( {2,3,4} \right){/tex}and {tex}B\left( {4,5,8} \right){/tex} at right angles.
solution_text: Since, the plane is bisecting perpendicularly the line joining the points {tex}A\left( {2,3,4} \right){/tex} and {tex}B\left( {4,5,8} \right){/tex} at right angles. So, mid-point of AB is {tex}\left( {\frac{{2 + 4}}{2},\frac{{3 + 5}}{2},\frac{{4 + 8}}{2}} \right){/tex} i.e., (3, 4, 6). Also, {tex}\vec N = \left( {4 - 2} \right)\hat i + \left( {5 - 3} \right)\hat j + \left( {8 - 4} \right)\hat k = 2\hat i + 2\hat j + 4\hat k{/tex} So, the required equation of the plane is {tex}\left( {\vec r - \vec a} \right).\vec N = 0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \left[ {\left( {x - 3} \right)\hat i + \left( {y - 4} \right)\hat j + \left( {z - 6} \right)\hat k} \right].\left( {2\hat i + 2\hat j + 4\hat k} \right) = 0{/tex} {tex}\left[ {\because \vec a = 3\hat i + 4\hat j + 6\hat k} \right]{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow 2x - 6 + 2y - 8 + 4z - 24 = 0{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow 2x + 2y + 4z = 38{/tex} {tex}\therefore x + y + 2z = 19{/tex}
solution_html:
Since, the  plane is bisecting perpendicularly  the line joining the points {tex}A\left( {2,3,4} \right){/tex} and {tex}B\left( {4,5,8} \right){/tex}at right angles.

So, mid-point of AB is {tex}\left( {\frac{{2 + 4}}{2},\frac{{3 + 5}}{2},\frac{{4 + 8}}{2}} \right){/tex} i.e., (3, 4, 6).

Also, {tex}\vec N = \left( {4 - 2} \right)\hat i + \left( {5 - 3} \right)\hat j + \left( {8 - 4} \right)\hat k = 2\hat i + 2\hat j + 4\hat k{/tex}

So, the required equation of the plane is {tex}\left( {\vec r - \vec a} \right).\vec N = 0{/tex}

{tex}\Rightarrow \left[ {\left( {x - 3} \right)\hat i + \left( {y - 4} \right)\hat j + \left( {z - 6} \right)\hat k} \right].\left( {2\hat i + 2\hat j + 4\hat k} \right) = 0{/tex}{tex}\left[ {\because \vec a = 3\hat i + 4\hat j + 6\hat k} \right]{/tex}

{tex} \Rightarrow 2x - 6 + 2y - 8 + 4z - 24 = 0{/tex}

{tex} \Rightarrow 2x + 2y + 4z = 38{/tex}

{tex}\therefore x + y + 2z = 19{/tex}

question_text: Find the equation of a plane which is at a distance {tex}3\sqrt 3 {/tex} units from origin and the normal to which is equally inclined to coordinate axes.
question_html: Find the equation of a plane which is at a distance {tex}3\sqrt 3 {/tex} units from origin and the normal to which is equally inclined to coordinate axes.
solution_text: Since, normal to the plane is equally inclined to the coordinate axis. Therefore, {tex}\cos \alpha = \cos \beta = \cos \gamma = \frac{1}{{\sqrt 3 }}{/tex} So, the normal is {tex}\overrightarrow N = \frac{1}{{\sqrt 3 }}\hat i + \frac{1}{{\sqrt 3 }}\hat j + \frac{1}{{\sqrt 3 }}\hat k{/tex} and plane is at a distance of {tex}3\sqrt 3 {/tex} units from origin. The equation of plane is {tex}\vec r.\hat N = 3\sqrt 3 \,\,\left[ {\because \hat N = \frac{{\vec N}}{{\left| N \right|}}} \right]{/tex} [Since, vector equation of the plane at a distance p from the origin is {tex}\vec r.\hat N = p{/tex} ] {tex} \Rightarrow \left( {x\hat i + y\hat j + z\hat k} \right).\frac{{\left( {\frac{1}{{\sqrt 3 }}\hat i + \frac{1}{{\sqrt 3 }}\hat j + \frac{1}{{\sqrt 3 }}\hat k} \right)}}{1} = 3\sqrt 3 {/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{x}{{\sqrt 3 }} + \frac{y}{{\sqrt 3 }} + \frac{z}{{\sqrt 3 }} = 3\sqrt 3 {/tex} {tex}\therefore x + y + z = 3\sqrt 3 .\sqrt 3 = 9{/tex} So, the required equation of plane is {tex}x + y + z = 9{/tex} .
solution_html:
Since, normal to the plane is equally inclined to the coordinate axis.

Therefore, {tex}\cos \alpha = \cos \beta = \cos \gamma = \frac{1}{{\sqrt 3 }}{/tex}

So, the normal is {tex}\overrightarrow N = \frac{1}{{\sqrt 3 }}\hat i + \frac{1}{{\sqrt 3 }}\hat j + \frac{1}{{\sqrt 3 }}\hat k{/tex} and plane is at a distance of {tex}3\sqrt 3 {/tex} units from origin.

The equation of plane is {tex}\vec r.\hat N = 3\sqrt 3 \,\,\left[ {\because \hat N = \frac{{\vec N}}{{\left| N \right|}}} \right]{/tex}

[Since, vector equation of the plane at a distance p from the origin is {tex}\vec r.\hat N = p{/tex}]

{tex} \Rightarrow \left( {x\hat i + y\hat j + z\hat k} \right).\frac{{\left( {\frac{1}{{\sqrt 3 }}\hat i + \frac{1}{{\sqrt 3 }}\hat j + \frac{1}{{\sqrt 3 }}\hat k} \right)}}{1} = 3\sqrt 3 {/tex}

{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{x}{{\sqrt 3 }} + \frac{y}{{\sqrt 3 }} + \frac{z}{{\sqrt 3 }} = 3\sqrt 3 {/tex}

{tex}\therefore x + y + z = 3\sqrt 3 .\sqrt 3 = 9{/tex}

So, the required equation of plane is {tex}x + y + z = 9{/tex}.

question_text: The foot of a perpendicular drawn from the point (-2, -1, -3) on a plane is (1, -3, 3). Find the equation of the plane.
question_html: The foot of a perpendicular drawn from the point (-2, -1, -3) on a plane is (1, -3, 3). Find the equation of the plane.
solution_text: We observe that the required plane passes through the point Q (1, - 3, 3) and is perpendicular to {tex}\overrightarrow { P Q } = ( \hat { i } - 3 \hat { j } + 3 \hat { k } ) - ( - 2 \hat { i } - {/tex} {tex}\hat { j } - 3 \hat { k } ) = 3 \hat { i } - 2 \hat { j } + 6 \hat { k }{/tex} . Thus, the required plane passes through the point Q having a position vector {tex}\vec { a } = \hat { i } - 3 \hat { j } + 3 \hat { k }{/tex} and is normal to {tex}\vec { n } = \overrightarrow { P Q } = 3 \hat { i } - 2 \hat { j } + 6 \hat { k }{/tex} . So, the vector equation of the plane is {tex}\vec { r } \cdot \vec { n } = \vec { a } \cdot \vec { n }{/tex} or, {tex}\vec { r } \cdot ( 3 \hat { i } - 2 \hat { j } + 6 \hat { k } ) = ( \hat { i } - 3 \hat { j } + 3 \hat { k } ) \cdot ( 3 \hat { i } - 2 \hat { j } + 6 \hat { k } ){/tex} or, {tex}\vec { r } \cdot ( 3 \hat { i } - 2 \hat { j } + 6 \hat { k } ){/tex} = 3 + 6 + 18 or, {tex}\vec { r } \cdot ( 3 \hat { i } - 2 \hat { j } + 6 \hat { k } ){/tex} = 27 The cartesian equation of the plane is {tex}( x \hat { i } + y \hat { j } + z \hat { k } ) \cdot ( 3 \hat { i } - 2 \hat { j } + 6 \hat { k } ){/tex} = 27 or, 3x - 2y + 6z = 27
solution_html:
We observe that the required plane passes through the point Q (1, - 3, 3) and is perpendicular to {tex}\overrightarrow { P Q } = ( \hat { i } - 3 \hat { j } + 3 \hat { k } ) - ( - 2 \hat { i } - {/tex}{tex}\hat { j } - 3 \hat { k } ) = 3 \hat { i } - 2 \hat { j } + 6 \hat { k }{/tex}. Thus, the required plane passes through the point Q having a position vector {tex}\vec { a } = \hat { i } - 3 \hat { j } + 3 \hat { k }{/tex} and is normal to {tex}\vec { n } = \overrightarrow { P Q } = 3 \hat { i } - 2 \hat { j } + 6 \hat { k }{/tex}. So, the vector equation of the plane is

{tex}\vec { r } \cdot \vec { n } = \vec { a } \cdot \vec { n }{/tex}
or, {tex}\vec { r } \cdot ( 3 \hat { i } - 2 \hat { j } + 6 \hat { k } ) = ( \hat { i } - 3 \hat { j } + 3 \hat { k } ) \cdot ( 3 \hat { i } - 2 \hat { j } + 6 \hat { k } ){/tex}
or, {tex}\vec { r } \cdot ( 3 \hat { i } - 2 \hat { j } + 6 \hat { k } ){/tex} = 3 + 6 + 18
or, {tex}\vec { r } \cdot ( 3 \hat { i } - 2 \hat { j } + 6 \hat { k } ){/tex} = 27
The cartesian equation of the plane is {tex}( x \hat { i } + y \hat { j } + z \hat { k } ) \cdot ( 3 \hat { i } - 2 \hat { j } + 6 \hat { k } ){/tex}= 27 or, 3x - 2y + 6z = 27

question_text: Find the equation of the plane through the points (2, 1, 0), (3, -2, -2) and (3, 1, 7).
question_html: Find the equation of the plane through the points (2, 1, 0), (3, -2, -2) and (3, 1, 7).
solution_text: We know that, the equation of a plane passing through three non-collinear points (x 1 , y 1 , z 1 ), (x 2 , y 2 , z 2 ) and (x 3 , y 3 , z 3 ) is {tex}\left|\begin{array}{ccc} {x-x_{1}} & {y-y_{1}} & {z-z_{1}} \\ {x_{2}-x_{1}} & {y_{2}-y_{1}} & {z_{2}-z_{1}} \\ {x_{3}-x_{1}} & {y_{3}-y_{1}} & {z_{3}-z_{1}} \end{array}\right|=0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow\left|\begin{array}{ccc} {x-2} & {y-1} & {z-0} \\ {3-2} & {-2-1} & {-2-0} \\ {3-2} & {1-1} & {7-0} \end{array}\right|=0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow\left|\begin{array}{ccc} {x-2} & {y-1} & {z} \\ {1} & {-3} & {-2} \\ {1} & {0} & {7} \end{array}\right|=0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (x - 2)(-21) - (y - 1)(9) + z(3) = 0 {tex}\therefore{/tex} equation of plane is 7x + 3y - z = 17
solution_html:
We know that, the equation of a plane passing through three non-collinear points (x1, y1, z1), (x2, y2, z2) and (x3, y3, z3) is
{tex}\left|\begin{array}{ccc} {x-x_{1}} & {y-y_{1}} & {z-z_{1}} \\ {x_{2}-x_{1}} & {y_{2}-y_{1}} & {z_{2}-z_{1}} \\ {x_{3}-x_{1}} & {y_{3}-y_{1}} & {z_{3}-z_{1}} \end{array}\right|=0{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow\left|\begin{array}{ccc} {x-2} & {y-1} & {z-0} \\ {3-2} & {-2-1} & {-2-0} \\ {3-2} & {1-1} & {7-0} \end{array}\right|=0{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow\left|\begin{array}{ccc} {x-2} & {y-1} & {z} \\ {1} & {-3} & {-2} \\ {1} & {0} & {7} \end{array}\right|=0{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (x - 2)(-21) - (y - 1)(9) + z(3) = 0
{tex}\therefore{/tex}  equation of plane is 7x + 3y - z = 17

question_text: Find the equations of the two lines through the origin which intersect the line {tex}\frac{x-3}{2}=\frac{y-3}{1}=\frac{z}{1}{/tex} at angles of {tex}\frac{\pi}{3}{/tex} each.
question_html: Find the equations of the two lines through the origin which intersect the line {tex}\frac{x-3}{2}=\frac{y-3}{1}=\frac{z}{1}{/tex} at angles of {tex}\frac{\pi}{3}{/tex} each.
solution_text: Given equation of the line is: {tex}\frac{x-3}{2}=\frac{y-3}{1}=\frac{z}{1}=\lambda{/tex} So, direction ratios of the line are (2, 1, 1) {tex}={/tex} (a 1 , b 1 , c 1 ) Any point on the given line is {tex}P(2 \lambda+3, \lambda+3, \lambda){/tex} So, direction ratios of OP are: {tex}(2 \lambda+3, \lambda+3, \lambda) \equiv\left(a_{2}, b_{2}, c_{2}\right){/tex} Now, angle between given line and OP is {tex}\frac{\pi}{3}{/tex} . {tex}\therefore\cos \frac{\pi}{3}=\frac{a_{1} a_{2}+b_{1} b_{2}+c_{1} c_{2}}{\sqrt{a_{1}^{2}+b_{1}^{2}+c_{1}^{2}} \sqrt{a_{2}^{2}+b_{2}^{2}+c_{2}^{2}}}{/tex} {tex}\therefore \frac{1}{2}=\frac{(4 \lambda+6)+(\lambda+3)+(\lambda)}{\sqrt{6} \sqrt{(2 \lambda+3)^{2}+(\lambda+3)^{2}+\lambda^{2}}}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac{\sqrt{6}}{2}=\frac{6 \lambda+9}{\sqrt{6 \lambda+18 \lambda+18}}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \sqrt{\lambda^{2}+3 \lambda+3}=2 \lambda+3{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \lambda^{2}+3 \lambda+3=4 \lambda^{2}+9+12 \lambda{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \lambda^{2}+3 \lambda+2=0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad(\lambda+1)(\lambda+2)=0{/tex} {tex}\therefore \lambda=-1,-2{/tex} So, the direction ratios are 1, 2, -1 and -1, 1, -2. Also, both the required lines passes through origin. So the equations of required lines are {tex}\frac{x}{1}=\frac{y}{2}=\frac{z}{-1} {/tex} and {tex}\frac{x}{-1}=\frac{y}{1}=\frac{z}{-2}{/tex}
solution_html:
Given equation of the line is: {tex}\frac{x-3}{2}=\frac{y-3}{1}=\frac{z}{1}=\lambda{/tex}
So, direction ratios of the line are (2, 1, 1) {tex}={/tex} (a1, b1, c1)
Any point on the given line is {tex}P(2 \lambda+3, \lambda+3, \lambda){/tex}
So, direction ratios of OP are:
{tex}(2 \lambda+3, \lambda+3, \lambda) \equiv\left(a_{2}, b_{2}, c_{2}\right){/tex}
Now, angle between given line and OP is {tex}\frac{\pi}{3}{/tex}.
{tex}\therefore\cos \frac{\pi}{3}=\frac{a_{1} a_{2}+b_{1} b_{2}+c_{1} c_{2}}{\sqrt{a_{1}^{2}+b_{1}^{2}+c_{1}^{2}} \sqrt{a_{2}^{2}+b_{2}^{2}+c_{2}^{2}}}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \frac{1}{2}=\frac{(4 \lambda+6)+(\lambda+3)+(\lambda)}{\sqrt{6} \sqrt{(2 \lambda+3)^{2}+(\lambda+3)^{2}+\lambda^{2}}}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac{\sqrt{6}}{2}=\frac{6 \lambda+9}{\sqrt{6 \lambda+18 \lambda+18}}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \sqrt{\lambda^{2}+3 \lambda+3}=2 \lambda+3{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \lambda^{2}+3 \lambda+3=4 \lambda^{2}+9+12 \lambda{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \lambda^{2}+3 \lambda+2=0{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad(\lambda+1)(\lambda+2)=0{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \lambda=-1,-2{/tex}
So, the direction ratios are 1, 2, -1 and -1, 1, -2.
Also, both the required lines passes through origin.
So the equations of required lines are {tex}\frac{x}{1}=\frac{y}{2}=\frac{z}{-1} {/tex} and {tex}\frac{x}{-1}=\frac{y}{1}=\frac{z}{-2}{/tex}

question_text: Find the angle between the lines whose direction cosines are given by the equation {tex}l + m + n = 0,{l^2} + {m^2} - {n^2} = 0{/tex} .
question_html: Find the angle between the lines whose direction cosines are given by the equation {tex}l + m + n = 0,{l^2} + {m^2} - {n^2} = 0{/tex}.
solution_text: Eliminating n from both the equations, we have {tex}{l^2} + {m^2} - {\left( {l - m} \right)^2} = 0{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow {l^2} + {m^2} - {l^2} - {m^2} + 2ml = 0{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow 2lm = 0 \Rightarrow lm = 0{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \left( { - m - n} \right)m = 0\,\,\left[ {\because l = - m - n} \right]{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \left( {m + n} \right)m = 0{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow m = - n , m = 0{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow l = 0,l = - n{/tex} Thus, Dr’s two lines are proportional to {tex}0, - n,n{/tex} and {tex} - n,0,n{/tex} i.e., {tex}0, - 1,1{/tex} and {tex} - 1,0,1{/tex} . So, the vectors parallel to these given lines are {tex}\vec a = - \hat j + \hat k{/tex} and {tex}\vec b = - \hat i + \hat k{/tex} Now, {tex}\cos \theta = \frac{{\vec a.\vec b}}{{\left| {\vec a} \right|\left| {\vec b} \right|}} = \frac{1}{{\sqrt 2 }}.\frac{1}{{\sqrt 2 }} \Rightarrow \cos \theta = \frac{1}{2}{/tex} {tex}\therefore \theta = \frac{\pi }{3}\,\,\left[ {\because \cos \frac{\pi }{3} = \frac{1}{2}} \right]{/tex}
solution_html:
Eliminating n from both the equations, we have

{tex}{l^2} + {m^2} - {\left( {l - m} \right)^2} = 0{/tex}

{tex} \Rightarrow {l^2} + {m^2} - {l^2} - {m^2} + 2ml = 0{/tex}

{tex} \Rightarrow 2lm = 0 \Rightarrow lm = 0{/tex}

{tex} \Rightarrow \left( { - m - n} \right)m = 0\,\,\left[ {\because l = - m - n} \right]{/tex}

{tex} \Rightarrow \left( {m + n} \right)m = 0{/tex}

{tex} \Rightarrow m = - n , m = 0{/tex}

{tex} \Rightarrow l = 0,l = - n{/tex}

Thus, Dr’s two lines are proportional to {tex}0, - n,n{/tex} and {tex} - n,0,n{/tex} i.e., {tex}0, - 1,1{/tex}and {tex} - 1,0,1{/tex}.

So, the vectors  parallel to these given lines are {tex}\vec a = - \hat j + \hat k{/tex} and {tex}\vec b = - \hat i + \hat k{/tex}

Now, {tex}\cos \theta = \frac{{\vec a.\vec b}}{{\left| {\vec a} \right|\left| {\vec b} \right|}} = \frac{1}{{\sqrt 2 }}.\frac{1}{{\sqrt 2 }} \Rightarrow \cos \theta = \frac{1}{2}{/tex}

{tex}\therefore \theta = \frac{\pi }{3}\,\,\left[ {\because \cos \frac{\pi }{3} = \frac{1}{2}} \right]{/tex}

question_text: If a variable line in two adjacent positions has direction cosines l, m, n and {tex}l+\delta l, m+\delta m, n+\delta n{/tex} , show that the small angle δθ between the two positions is given by {tex}\delta \theta^{2}=\delta l^{2}+\delta m^{2}+\delta n^{2}{/tex} .
question_html: If a variable line in two adjacent positions has direction cosines l, m, n and {tex}l+\delta l, m+\delta m, n+\delta n{/tex}, show that the small angle δθ between the two positions is given by {tex}\delta \theta^{2}=\delta l^{2}+\delta m^{2}+\delta n^{2}{/tex}.
solution_text: We have l, m, n and {tex}l+\delta, m+\delta m, n+\delta n{/tex} as direction cosines of a variable line in two different positions. {tex}\therefore{/tex} l 2 + m 2 + n 2 = 1 ...(i) and {tex}(l+\delta l)^{2}+(m+\delta m)^{2}+(n+\delta n)^{2}=1{/tex} ...(ii) {tex}\Rightarrow l^{2}+m^{2}+n^{2}+n^{2}+\delta m^{2}+\delta n^{2}+2(l \delta l+m \delta m+n \delta n)=1 {/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \delta l^{2}+\delta m^{2}+\delta n^{2}=-2(l \delta l+m \delta m+n \delta n) \quad\left[\because l^{2}+m^{2}+n^{2}=1\right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow l \delta l+m \delta m+n \delta n=\frac{-1}{2}\left(\delta l^{2}+\delta m^{2}+\delta n^{2}\right){/tex} ...(iii) Now {tex}\overrightarrow {a}{/tex} and {tex}\overrightarrow {b}{/tex} are unit vectors along a line with direction cosines l, m, n and {tex}(l+\delta),(m+\delta m),(n+\delta n){/tex} , respectively. {tex}\therefore \quad \vec{a}=l\hat{i}+m \hat{j}+n \hat{k}{/tex} and {tex}\vec{b}=(l+\delta l) \hat{i}+(m+\delta m) \hat{j}+(n+\delta n) \hat{k}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \cos \delta \theta=l(l+\delta h)+m(m+\delta m)+n(n+\delta n){/tex} {tex}=\left(l^{2}+m^{2}+n^{2}\right)+(l \delta l+m \delta m+n \delta n){/tex} {tex}=1-\frac{1}{2}\left(\delta l^{2}+\delta m^{2}+\delta n^{2}\right){/tex} [Using Eq. (iii)] {tex}\Rightarrow \quad 2(1-\cos \delta \theta)=\left(\delta l^{2}+\delta m^{2}=\delta n^{2}\right){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad 2\times2 \sin ^{2} \frac{\delta \theta}{2}=\delta l^{2}+\delta m^{2}+\delta n^{2}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad 4\left(\frac{\delta \theta}{2}\right)^{2}=\delta l^{2}+\delta m^{2}+\delta n^{2}{/tex} {tex}\left[\text { since } \frac{\delta \theta}{2} \text { is small, } \sin \frac{\delta \theta}{2}=\frac{\delta \theta}{2}\right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \delta \theta^{2}=\delta l^{2}+\delta m^{2}+\delta n^{2}{/tex}
solution_html:
We have l, m, n and {tex}l+\delta, m+\delta m, n+\delta n{/tex} as direction cosines of a variable line in two different positions.
{tex}\therefore{/tex} l2 + m2 + n2 = 1 ...(i)
and {tex}(l+\delta l)^{2}+(m+\delta m)^{2}+(n+\delta n)^{2}=1{/tex} ...(ii)
{tex}\Rightarrow l^{2}+m^{2}+n^{2}+n^{2}+\delta m^{2}+\delta n^{2}+2(l \delta l+m \delta m+n \delta n)=1 {/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \delta l^{2}+\delta m^{2}+\delta n^{2}=-2(l \delta l+m \delta m+n \delta n) \quad\left[\because l^{2}+m^{2}+n^{2}=1\right]{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow l \delta l+m \delta m+n \delta n=\frac{-1}{2}\left(\delta l^{2}+\delta m^{2}+\delta n^{2}\right){/tex} ...(iii)
Now {tex}\overrightarrow {a}{/tex} and {tex}\overrightarrow {b}{/tex} are unit vectors along a line with direction cosines l, m, n and {tex}(l+\delta),(m+\delta m),(n+\delta n){/tex}, respectively.
{tex}\therefore \quad \vec{a}=l\hat{i}+m \hat{j}+n \hat{k}{/tex} and {tex}\vec{b}=(l+\delta l) \hat{i}+(m+\delta m) \hat{j}+(n+\delta n) \hat{k}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \cos \delta \theta=l(l+\delta h)+m(m+\delta m)+n(n+\delta n){/tex} {tex}=\left(l^{2}+m^{2}+n^{2}\right)+(l \delta l+m \delta m+n \delta n){/tex}
{tex}=1-\frac{1}{2}\left(\delta l^{2}+\delta m^{2}+\delta n^{2}\right){/tex} [Using Eq. (iii)]
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad 2(1-\cos \delta \theta)=\left(\delta l^{2}+\delta m^{2}=\delta n^{2}\right){/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad 2\times2 \sin ^{2} \frac{\delta \theta}{2}=\delta l^{2}+\delta m^{2}+\delta n^{2}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad 4\left(\frac{\delta \theta}{2}\right)^{2}=\delta l^{2}+\delta m^{2}+\delta n^{2}{/tex} {tex}\left[\text { since } \frac{\delta \theta}{2} \text { is small, } \sin \frac{\delta \theta}{2}=\frac{\delta \theta}{2}\right]{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \delta \theta^{2}=\delta l^{2}+\delta m^{2}+\delta n^{2}{/tex}

question_text: O is the origin and A is (a, b, c). Find the direction cosines of the line OA and the equation of plane through A at right angle to OA.
question_html: O is the origin and A is (a, b, c). Find the direction cosines of the line OA and the equation of plane through A at right angle to OA.
solution_text: DC’s of line OA are {tex}\frac{a}{{\sqrt {{a^2} + {b^2} + {c^2}} }},\frac{b}{{\sqrt {{a^2} + {b^2} + {c^2}} }},\frac{c}{{\sqrt {{a^2} + {b^2} + {c^2}} }}{/tex} Also, {tex}\vec n = \overrightarrow {OA} = \vec a = a\hat i + b\hat j + c\hat k{/tex} The equation of plane passes through (a, b, c) and perpendicular to OA is given by {tex}\left[ {\vec r - \vec a} \right].\vec n = 0{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \vec r.\vec n = \vec a.\vec n{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \left[ {\left( {x\hat i + y\hat j + z\hat k} \right).\left( {a\hat i + b\hat j + c\hat k} \right)} \right] = \left( {a\hat i + b\hat j + c\hat k} \right).\left( {a\hat i + b\hat j + c\hat k} \right){/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow ax + by + cz = {a^2} + {b^2} + {c^2}{/tex}
solution_html:
DC’s of line OA are {tex}\frac{a}{{\sqrt {{a^2} + {b^2} + {c^2}} }},\frac{b}{{\sqrt {{a^2} + {b^2} + {c^2}} }},\frac{c}{{\sqrt {{a^2} + {b^2} + {c^2}} }}{/tex}

Also, {tex}\vec n = \overrightarrow {OA} = \vec a = a\hat i + b\hat j + c\hat k{/tex}

The equation of plane passes through (a, b, c) and perpendicular to OA is given by

{tex}\left[ {\vec r - \vec a} \right].\vec n = 0{/tex}

{tex} \Rightarrow \vec r.\vec n = \vec a.\vec n{/tex}

{tex}\Rightarrow \left[ {\left( {x\hat i + y\hat j + z\hat k} \right).\left( {a\hat i + b\hat j + c\hat k} \right)} \right] = \left( {a\hat i + b\hat j + c\hat k} \right).\left( {a\hat i + b\hat j + c\hat k} \right){/tex}

{tex} \Rightarrow ax + by + cz = {a^2} + {b^2} + {c^2}{/tex}

question_text: Two systems of rectangular axis have the same origin. If a plane cuts them at distances a, b, c and a′, b′, c′, respectively, from the origin, prove that {tex}\frac{1}{a^{2}}+\frac{1}{b^{2}}+\frac{1}{c^{2}}=\frac{1}{a^{\prime2}}+\frac{1}{b^{\prime 2}}+\frac{1}{c^{\prime 2}}{/tex}
question_html: Two systems of rectangular axis have the same origin. If a plane cuts them at distances a, b, c and a′, b′, c′, respectively, from the origin, prove that {tex}\frac{1}{a^{2}}+\frac{1}{b^{2}}+\frac{1}{c^{2}}=\frac{1}{a^{\prime2}}+\frac{1}{b^{\prime 2}}+\frac{1}{c^{\prime 2}}{/tex}
solution_text: Consider OX, OY, OZ, and OX', OY', OZ' are two systems of rectangular axes. Let their corresponding equation of plane be {tex}\frac{x}{a}+\frac{y}{b}+\frac{z}{c}=1{/tex} ...(i) and {tex}\frac{x}{a^{\prime}}+\frac{y}{b^{\prime}}+\frac{z}{c^{\prime}}=1{/tex} ...(ii) Also, the length of the perpendicular from origin to Eqs. (i) and (ii) must be the same. {tex}\therefore \quad \frac{\frac{0}{a}+\frac{0}{b}+\frac{0}{c}-1}{\sqrt{\frac{1}{a^{2}}+\frac{1}{b^{2}}+\frac{1}{c^{2}}}}=\frac{\frac{0}{a^{\prime}}+\frac{0}{b^{\prime}}+\frac{0}{c^{\prime}}-1}{\sqrt{\frac{1}{a^{\prime2}}+\frac{1}{b^{\prime 2}}+\frac{1}{c^{\prime 2}}}}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac{1}{a^{2}}+\frac{1}{b^{2}}+\frac{1}{c^{2}}=\frac{1}{a^{\prime2}}+\frac{1}{b^{\prime 2}}+\frac{1}{c^{\prime 2}}{/tex}
solution_html:
Consider OX, OY, OZ, and OX', OY', OZ' are two systems of rectangular axes.
Let their corresponding equation of plane be
{tex}\frac{x}{a}+\frac{y}{b}+\frac{z}{c}=1{/tex} ...(i)
and {tex}\frac{x}{a^{\prime}}+\frac{y}{b^{\prime}}+\frac{z}{c^{\prime}}=1{/tex} ...(ii)
Also, the length of the perpendicular from origin to Eqs. (i) and (ii) must be the same.
{tex}\therefore \quad \frac{\frac{0}{a}+\frac{0}{b}+\frac{0}{c}-1}{\sqrt{\frac{1}{a^{2}}+\frac{1}{b^{2}}+\frac{1}{c^{2}}}}=\frac{\frac{0}{a^{\prime}}+\frac{0}{b^{\prime}}+\frac{0}{c^{\prime}}-1}{\sqrt{\frac{1}{a^{\prime2}}+\frac{1}{b^{\prime 2}}+\frac{1}{c^{\prime 2}}}}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac{1}{a^{2}}+\frac{1}{b^{2}}+\frac{1}{c^{2}}=\frac{1}{a^{\prime2}}+\frac{1}{b^{\prime 2}}+\frac{1}{c^{\prime 2}}{/tex}

question_text: Find the co-ordinates of the foot of the perpendicular drawn from the point {tex}(2,3,-8){/tex} to the line {tex}\frac{4-x}{2}=\frac{y}{6}=\frac{1-z}{3}{/tex} . Also, find the perpendicular distance of the given point from the line.
question_html:
Find the co-ordinates of the foot of the perpendicular drawn from the point {tex}(2,3,-8){/tex} to the line {tex}\frac{4-x}{2}=\frac{y}{6}=\frac{1-z}{3}{/tex}.
Also, find the perpendicular distance of the given point from the line.
solution_text: We have equation of line is {tex}\frac{{4 - x}}{2} = \frac{y}{6} = \frac{{1 - z}}{3}{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \frac{{x - 4}}{{ - 2}} = \frac{y}{6} = \frac{{z - 1}}{{ - 3}} = \lambda {/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow x = - 2\lambda + 4,y = 6\lambda {/tex} and {tex}z = - 3\lambda + 1{/tex} Let the coordinates of L be {tex}(4 - 2\lambda ,6\lambda ,1 - 3\lambda ){/tex} , then, direction ratios of PL are proportional to {tex}(4 - 2\lambda - 2,6\lambda - 3,1 - 3\lambda + 8){/tex} i.e., {tex}(2 - 2\lambda ,6\lambda - 3,9 - 3\lambda ){/tex} . Also, direction ratios are proportional to -2, 6, -3. Since, PL is perpendicular to give line. {tex}\therefore - 2(2 - 2\lambda ) + 6(6\lambda - 3) - 3(9 - 3\lambda ) = 0{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow - 4 + 4\lambda + 36\lambda - 18 - 27 + 9\lambda = 0{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow 49\lambda = 49 \Rightarrow \lambda = 1{/tex} So, the coordinates of L are {tex}\left( {4 - 2\lambda ,6\lambda ,1 - 3\lambda } \right){/tex} i.e., (2, 6, -2). Also, length of PL {tex} = \sqrt {{{(2 - 2)}^2} + {{(6 - 3)}^2} + {{( - 2 + 8)}^2}} {/tex} {tex} = \sqrt {0 + 9 + 36} = 3\sqrt 5 units{/tex}
solution_html:
We have equation of line is {tex}\frac{{4 - x}}{2} = \frac{y}{6} = \frac{{1 - z}}{3}{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow \frac{{x - 4}}{{ - 2}} = \frac{y}{6} = \frac{{z - 1}}{{ - 3}} = \lambda {/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow x = - 2\lambda + 4,y = 6\lambda {/tex} and {tex}z = - 3\lambda + 1{/tex}

Let the coordinates of L be {tex}(4 - 2\lambda ,6\lambda ,1 - 3\lambda ){/tex}, then, direction ratios of PL are proportional to {tex}(4 - 2\lambda - 2,6\lambda - 3,1 - 3\lambda + 8){/tex} i.e., {tex}(2 - 2\lambda ,6\lambda - 3,9 - 3\lambda ){/tex}.
Also, direction ratios are proportional to -2, 6, -3. Since, PL is perpendicular to give line.
{tex}\therefore - 2(2 - 2\lambda ) + 6(6\lambda - 3) - 3(9 - 3\lambda ) = 0{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow - 4 + 4\lambda + 36\lambda - 18 - 27 + 9\lambda = 0{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow 49\lambda = 49 \Rightarrow \lambda = 1{/tex}
So, the coordinates of L are {tex}\left( {4 - 2\lambda ,6\lambda ,1 - 3\lambda } \right){/tex} i.e., (2, 6, -2).

Also, length of PL {tex} = \sqrt {{{(2 - 2)}^2} + {{(6 - 3)}^2} + {{( - 2 + 8)}^2}} {/tex}
{tex} = \sqrt {0 + 9 + 36} = 3\sqrt 5 units{/tex}

question_html: Find the distance of a point (2, 4, –1) from the line {tex}\frac{{x + 5}}{1} = \frac{{y + 3}}{4} = \frac{{z - 6}}{{ - 9}}{/tex}.
solution_text: We have equation of the line as {tex}\frac{{x + 5}}{1} = \frac{{y + 3}}{4} = \frac{{z - 6}}{{ - 9}} = \lambda {/tex} . {tex} \Rightarrow x = \lambda - 5,y = 4\lambda - 3,z = 6 - 9\lambda {/tex} Let the coordinates of L be {tex}\left( {\lambda - 5,4\lambda - 3,6 - 9\lambda } \right){/tex} , then Dr’s of PL are {tex}\left( {\lambda - 7,4\lambda - 7,7 - 9\lambda } \right){/tex} . Also, the direction ratios of given line are proportional to 1, 4, -9. Since, P L is perpendicular to the given line. {tex}\therefore \left( {\lambda - 7} \right) \cdot 1 + (4\lambda - 7) \cdot 4 + (7 - 9\lambda ) \cdot ( - 9) = 0{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \lambda - 7 + 16\lambda - 28 + 81\lambda - 63 = 0{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow 98\lambda = 98 \Rightarrow \lambda = 1{/tex} So, the coordinates of L are (-4, 1, -3). {tex}\therefore {/tex} Required distance, PL {tex} = \sqrt {{{( - 4 - 2)}^2} + {{(1 - 4)}^2} + {{( - 3 + 1)}^2}} {/tex} {tex} = \sqrt {36 + 9 + 4} = 7\;units{/tex}

question_html: Find the length and the foot of perpendicular from the point {tex}\left( {1,\frac{3}{2},2} \right){/tex} to the plane 2x - 2y + 4z + 5 = 0.
solution_text: Equation of the given plane is 2x - 2y + 4z + 5 = 0 ........ (i) {tex} \Rightarrow \overrightarrow n = 2\hat i - 2\hat j + 4\hat k{/tex} So, the equation of line through {tex}\left( {1,\frac{3}{2},2} \right){/tex} and parallel to {tex}\overrightarrow n {/tex} is given by {tex}\frac{{x - 1}}{2} = \frac{{y-\frac{{3}}{2}}}{{ - 2}} = \frac{{z - 2}}{4} = \lambda {/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow x = 2\lambda + 1,y = - 2\lambda + \frac{3}{2}{/tex} and {tex}z = 4\lambda + 2{/tex} If this point lies on the given plane, then {tex}2\left( {2\lambda + 1} \right) - 2\left( { - 2\lambda + \frac{3}{2}} \right) + 4\left( {4\lambda + 2} \right) + 5 = 0{/tex} [using Eq. (i)] {tex} \Rightarrow 4\lambda + 2 + 4\lambda - 3 + 16\lambda + 8 + 5 = 0{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow 24\lambda = - 12 \Rightarrow \lambda = \frac{{ - 1}}{2}{/tex} {tex}\therefore {/tex} Required foot of perpendicular {tex} = \left[ {2 \times \left( {\frac{{ - 1}}{2}} \right) + 1, - 2 \times \left( {\frac{{ - 1}}{2}} \right) + \frac{3}{2},4 \times \left( {\frac{{ - 1}}{2}} \right) + 2} \right]{/tex} i.e., {tex}\left( {0,\frac{5}{2},0} \right){/tex} {tex}\therefore {/tex} Required length of perpendicular {tex} = \sqrt {{{(1 - 0)}^2} + {{\left( {\frac{3}{2} - \frac{5}{2}} \right)}^2} + {{(2 - 0)}^2}} {/tex} {tex} = \sqrt {1 + 1 + 4} = \sqrt 6 \;units{/tex}
solution_html:
Equation of the given plane is 2x - 2y + 4z + 5 = 0 ........ (i)
{tex} \Rightarrow \overrightarrow n = 2\hat i - 2\hat j + 4\hat k{/tex}
So, the equation of line through {tex}\left( {1,\frac{3}{2},2} \right){/tex} and parallel to {tex}\overrightarrow n {/tex} is given by
{tex}\frac{{x - 1}}{2} = \frac{{y-\frac{{3}}{2}}}{{ - 2}} = \frac{{z - 2}}{4} = \lambda {/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow x = 2\lambda + 1,y = - 2\lambda + \frac{3}{2}{/tex} and {tex}z = 4\lambda + 2{/tex}
If this point lies on the given plane, then
{tex}2\left( {2\lambda + 1} \right) - 2\left( { - 2\lambda + \frac{3}{2}} \right) + 4\left( {4\lambda + 2} \right) + 5 = 0{/tex}[using Eq. (i)]
{tex} \Rightarrow 4\lambda + 2 + 4\lambda - 3 + 16\lambda + 8 + 5 = 0{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow 24\lambda = - 12 \Rightarrow \lambda = \frac{{ - 1}}{2}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore {/tex} Required foot of perpendicular
{tex} = \left[ {2 \times \left( {\frac{{ - 1}}{2}} \right) + 1, - 2 \times \left( {\frac{{ - 1}}{2}} \right) + \frac{3}{2},4 \times \left( {\frac{{ - 1}}{2}} \right) + 2} \right]{/tex}i.e., {tex}\left( {0,\frac{5}{2},0} \right){/tex}
{tex}\therefore {/tex} Required length of perpendicular {tex} = \sqrt {{{(1 - 0)}^2} + {{\left( {\frac{3}{2} - \frac{5}{2}} \right)}^2} + {{(2 - 0)}^2}} {/tex}
{tex} = \sqrt {1 + 1 + 4} = \sqrt 6 \;units{/tex}

question_text: Find the equations of the line passing through the point (3, 0, 1) and parallel to the planes x + 2y = 0 and 3y - z = 0.
question_html: Find the equations of the line passing through the point (3, 0, 1) and parallel to the planes x + 2y = 0 and 3y - z = 0.
solution_text: Equation of the two planes are x + 2y = 0 and 3y - z = 0. Let {tex}{\overrightarrow n _1}{/tex} and {tex}{\overrightarrow n _2}{/tex} be the normal to the two planes, respectively. {tex}\therefore {\overrightarrow n _1} = \hat i + 2\hat j{/tex} and {tex}{\overrightarrow n _2} = 3\hat j - \hat k{/tex} Since required line is parallel to the given two planes. Therefore, {tex}\overrightarrow b = {\overrightarrow n _1} \times {\overrightarrow n _2} = \left| {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {\hat i}&{\hat j}&{\hat k} \\ 1&2&0 \\ 0&3&{ - 1} \end{array}} \right|{/tex} {tex} = \hat i( - 2) - \hat j( - 1) + \hat k(3){/tex} {tex} = - 2\hat i + \hat j + 3\hat k{/tex} So, the equation of the line through the point (3, 0, 1) and parallel to the given two planes is {tex}(x - 3)\hat i + (y - 0)\hat j + (z - 1)\hat k + \lambda ( - 2\hat i + \hat j + 3\hat k){/tex} {tex}=(x - 3)\hat i + y\hat j + (z - 1)\hat k + \lambda ( - 2\hat i + \hat j + 3\hat k){/tex}
solution_html:
Equation of the two planes are x + 2y = 0 and 3y - z = 0.
Let {tex}{\overrightarrow n _1}{/tex} and {tex}{\overrightarrow n _2}{/tex} be  the normal to the two planes, respectively.
{tex}\therefore {\overrightarrow n _1} = \hat i + 2\hat j{/tex} and {tex}{\overrightarrow n _2} = 3\hat j - \hat k{/tex}
Since required line is parallel to the given two planes.
Therefore, {tex}\overrightarrow b = {\overrightarrow n _1} \times {\overrightarrow n _2} = \left| {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {\hat i}&{\hat j}&{\hat k} \\ 1&2&0 \\ 0&3&{ - 1} \end{array}} \right|{/tex}
{tex} = \hat i( - 2) - \hat j( - 1) + \hat k(3){/tex}
{tex} = - 2\hat i + \hat j + 3\hat k{/tex}
So, the equation of the line through the point (3, 0, 1) and parallel to the given two planes is 
{tex}(x - 3)\hat i + (y - 0)\hat j + (z - 1)\hat k + \lambda ( - 2\hat i + \hat j + 3\hat k){/tex}
{tex}=(x - 3)\hat i + y\hat j + (z - 1)\hat k + \lambda ( - 2\hat i + \hat j + 3\hat k){/tex}

question_text: Find the equation of the plane through the points (2, 1, -1) and (-1, 3, 4), and perpendicular to the plane x - 2y + 4z = 10.
question_html: Find the equation of the plane through the points (2, 1, -1) and (-1, 3, 4), and perpendicular to the plane x - 2y + 4z = 10.
solution_text: The equation of the plane passing through (2, 1, -1) is a(x - 2) + b(y - 1) + c(z + 1) = 0 ...... (i) Since, this passes through (-1, 3, 4) {tex}\therefore a( - 1 - 2) + b(3 - 1) + c(4 + 1) = 0{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow - 3a + 2b + 5c = 0{/tex} ..... (ii) Since, the plane (i) is perpendicular to the plane x - 2y + 4z = 10. {tex}\therefore 1 \cdot a - 2 \cdot b + 4 \cdot c = 0{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow a - 2b + 4c = 0{/tex} ........ (iii) On solving Eqs. (ii) and (iii), we get {tex}\frac{a}{{8 + 10}} = \frac{{ - b}}{{ - 17}} = \frac{c}{4} = \lambda {/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow a = 18\lambda ,{/tex} {tex}b = 17\lambda ,\lambda = 4\lambda {/tex} From Eq. (i), {tex}18\lambda (x - 2) + 17\lambda (y - 1) + 4\lambda (z + 1) = 0{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow 18x - 36 + 17y - 17 + 4z + 4 = 0{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow 18x + 17y + 4z - 49 = 0{/tex} {tex}\therefore {/tex} 18x + 17y + 4z = 49
solution_html:
The equation of the plane passing through (2, 1, -1) is
a(x - 2) + b(y - 1) + c(z + 1) = 0 ...... (i)
Since, this passes through (-1, 3, 4)
{tex}\therefore a( - 1 - 2) + b(3 - 1) + c(4 + 1) = 0{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow - 3a + 2b + 5c = 0{/tex} ..... (ii)
Since, the plane (i) is perpendicular to the plane x - 2y + 4z = 10.
{tex}\therefore 1 \cdot a - 2 \cdot b + 4 \cdot c = 0{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow a - 2b + 4c = 0{/tex} ........ (iii)
On solving Eqs. (ii) and (iii), we get
{tex}\frac{a}{{8 + 10}} = \frac{{ - b}}{{ - 17}} = \frac{c}{4} = \lambda {/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow a = 18\lambda ,{/tex} {tex}b = 17\lambda ,\lambda = 4\lambda {/tex}
From Eq. (i),
{tex}18\lambda (x - 2) + 17\lambda (y - 1) + 4\lambda (z + 1) = 0{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow 18x - 36 + 17y - 17 + 4z + 4 = 0{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow 18x + 17y + 4z - 49 = 0{/tex}
{tex}\therefore {/tex} 18x + 17y + 4z = 49

question_text: Find the shortest distance between the lines given by {tex}\overrightarrow r = (8 + 3\lambda )\hat i - (9 + 16\lambda )\hat j + (10 + 7\lambda )\hat k{/tex} and {tex}\overrightarrow r = 15\hat i + 29\hat j + 5\hat k + \mu (3\hat i + 8\hat j - 5\hat k).{/tex}
question_html: Find the shortest distance between the lines given by {tex}\overrightarrow r = (8 + 3\lambda )\hat i - (9 + 16\lambda )\hat j + (10 + 7\lambda )\hat k{/tex} and {tex}\overrightarrow r = 15\hat i + 29\hat j + 5\hat k + \mu (3\hat i + 8\hat j - 5\hat k).{/tex}
solution_text: We have, {tex}\overrightarrow r = (8 + 3\lambda )\hat i + ( - 9 - 16\lambda )\hat j + (10 + 7\lambda )\hat k{/tex} {tex} = 8\hat i - 9\hat j + 10\hat k + 3\lambda \hat i - 16\lambda \hat j + 7\lambda \hat k{/tex} {tex} = 8\hat i - 9\hat j + 10\hat k + \lambda (3\hat i - 16\hat j + 7\hat k){/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow {\overrightarrow a _1} = 8\hat i - 9\hat j + 10\hat k{/tex} and {tex}{\overrightarrow b _1} = 3\hat i - 16\hat j + 7\hat k{/tex} .... (i) Also, {tex}\overrightarrow r = 15\hat i + 29\hat j + 5\hat k + \mu (3\hat i + 8\hat j - 5\hat k){/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow {\overrightarrow a _2} = 15\hat i + 29\hat j + 5\hat k{/tex} and {tex}\overrightarrow {{b_2}} = 3\hat i + 8\hat j - 5\hat k{/tex} ....... (ii) Now, shortest distance between two lines is given by {tex}\left| {\frac{{({{\overrightarrow b }_1} \times {{\overrightarrow b }_2}) \cdot ({{\overrightarrow a }_1} - {{\overrightarrow a }_2})}}{{\left| {{{\overrightarrow b }_1} \times {{\overrightarrow b }_2}} \right|}}} \right|{/tex} {tex}\therefore {\overrightarrow b _1} \times {\overrightarrow b _2} = \left| {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} \hat i&\hat j&\hat k \\ 3&{ - 16}&7 \\ 3&8&{ - 5} \end{array}} \right|{/tex} {tex} = \hat i(80 - 56) - \hat j( - 15 - 21) + \hat k(24 + 48){/tex} {tex} = 24\hat i + 36\hat j + 72\hat k{/tex} Now, {tex}\left| {{{\overrightarrow b }_1} \times {{\overrightarrow b }_2}} \right| = \sqrt {{{(24)}^2} + {{(36)}^2} + {{(72)}^2}} {/tex} {tex} = 12\sqrt {{2^2} + {3^2} + {6^2}} = 84{/tex} And {tex}\left( {{{\overrightarrow a }_2} - {{\overrightarrow a }_1}} \right) = (15 - 8)\hat i + (29 + 9)\hat j + (5 - 10)\hat k{/tex} {tex} = 7\hat i + 38\hat j - 5k{/tex} {tex}\therefore {/tex} Shortest distance {tex} = \left| {\frac{{(24i + 36\hat j + 72\hat k) \cdot (7\hat i + 38\hat j - 5\hat k)}}{{84}}} \right|{/tex} {tex} = \left| {\frac{{168 + 1368 - 360}}{{84}}} \right| = \left| {\frac{{1176}}{{84}}} \right| = 14{/tex} units
solution_html:
We have, {tex}\overrightarrow r = (8 + 3\lambda )\hat i + ( - 9 - 16\lambda )\hat j + (10 + 7\lambda )\hat k{/tex}
{tex} = 8\hat i - 9\hat j + 10\hat k + 3\lambda \hat i - 16\lambda \hat j + 7\lambda \hat k{/tex}
{tex} = 8\hat i - 9\hat j + 10\hat k + \lambda (3\hat i - 16\hat j + 7\hat k){/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow {\overrightarrow a _1} = 8\hat i - 9\hat j + 10\hat k{/tex} and {tex}{\overrightarrow b _1} = 3\hat i - 16\hat j + 7\hat k{/tex} .... (i)
Also, {tex}\overrightarrow r = 15\hat i + 29\hat j + 5\hat k + \mu (3\hat i + 8\hat j - 5\hat k){/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow {\overrightarrow a _2} = 15\hat i + 29\hat j + 5\hat k{/tex} and {tex}\overrightarrow {{b_2}} = 3\hat i + 8\hat j - 5\hat k{/tex} ....... (ii)
Now, shortest distance between two lines is given by {tex}\left| {\frac{{({{\overrightarrow b }_1} \times {{\overrightarrow b }_2}) \cdot ({{\overrightarrow a }_1} - {{\overrightarrow a }_2})}}{{\left| {{{\overrightarrow b }_1} \times {{\overrightarrow b }_2}} \right|}}} \right|{/tex}
{tex}\therefore {\overrightarrow b _1} \times {\overrightarrow b _2} = \left| {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} \hat i&\hat j&\hat k \\ 3&{ - 16}&7 \\ 3&8&{ - 5} \end{array}} \right|{/tex}
{tex} = \hat i(80 - 56) - \hat j( - 15 - 21) + \hat k(24 + 48){/tex}
{tex} = 24\hat i + 36\hat j + 72\hat k{/tex}
Now, {tex}\left| {{{\overrightarrow b }_1} \times {{\overrightarrow b }_2}} \right| = \sqrt {{{(24)}^2} + {{(36)}^2} + {{(72)}^2}} {/tex}
{tex} = 12\sqrt {{2^2} + {3^2} + {6^2}} = 84{/tex}
And {tex}\left( {{{\overrightarrow a }_2} - {{\overrightarrow a }_1}} \right) = (15 - 8)\hat i + (29 + 9)\hat j + (5 - 10)\hat k{/tex}
{tex} = 7\hat i + 38\hat j - 5k{/tex}
{tex}\therefore {/tex} Shortest distance {tex} = \left| {\frac{{(24i + 36\hat j + 72\hat k) \cdot (7\hat i + 38\hat j - 5\hat k)}}{{84}}} \right|{/tex}
{tex} = \left| {\frac{{168 + 1368 - 360}}{{84}}} \right| = \left| {\frac{{1176}}{{84}}} \right| = 14{/tex} units

question_text: Find the equation of the plane which is perpendicular to the plane 5x + 3y + 6z + 8 = 0 and which contains the line of intersection of the planes x + 2y + 3z - 4 = 0 and 2x + y - z + 5 = 0.
question_html: Find the equation of the plane which is perpendicular to the plane 5x + 3y + 6z + 8 = 0 and which contains the line of intersection of the planes x + 2y + 3z - 4 = 0 and 2x + y - z + 5 = 0.
solution_text: The equation of a plane through the line of intersection of the planes x + 2y + 3z - 4 = 0 and 2x + y - z + 5 = 0 is (x + 2y + 3z - 4) + {tex}\lambda {/tex} (2x + y - z + 5) = 0 {tex} \Rightarrow x(1 + 2\lambda ) + y (2 + \lambda ) + z( - \lambda + 3) - 4 + 5\lambda = 0{/tex} ..... (i) Also, this is perpendicular to the plane 5x + 3y + 6z + 8 = 0. {tex}\therefore 5(1 + 2\lambda ) + 3(2 + \lambda ) + 6(3 - \lambda ) = 0{/tex} [ {tex}\because {/tex} a 1 a 2 + b 1 b 2 + c 1 c 2 = 0] {tex} \Rightarrow 5 + 10\lambda + 6 + 3\lambda + 18 - 6\lambda = 0{/tex} {tex}\therefore \lambda = - \frac{{29}}{7}{/tex} From Eq. (i), {tex}x\left[ {1 + 2\left( {\frac{{ - 29}}{7}} \right)} \right] + y\left( {2 - \frac{{29}}{7}} \right) + z\left( {\frac{{29}}{7} + 3} \right) - 4 + 5\left( {\frac{{ - 29}}{7}} \right) = 0{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow {/tex} x(7 - 58) + y(14 - 29) + z(29 + 21) - 28 - 145 = 0 {tex} \Rightarrow {/tex} -51x - 15y + 50z - 173 = 0 So, the required equation of plane is 51x + 15y - 50z + 173 = 0.
solution_html:
The equation of a plane through the line of intersection of the planes x + 2y + 3z - 4 = 0 and 2x + y - z + 5 = 0 is
(x + 2y + 3z - 4) + {tex}\lambda {/tex}(2x + y - z + 5) = 0
{tex} \Rightarrow x(1 + 2\lambda ) + y (2 + \lambda ) + z( - \lambda + 3) - 4 + 5\lambda = 0{/tex}..... (i)
Also, this is perpendicular to the plane 5x + 3y + 6z + 8 = 0.
{tex}\therefore 5(1 + 2\lambda ) + 3(2 + \lambda ) + 6(3 - \lambda ) = 0{/tex}[{tex}\because {/tex} a1a2 + b1b2 + c1c2 = 0]
{tex} \Rightarrow 5 + 10\lambda + 6 + 3\lambda + 18 - 6\lambda = 0{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \lambda = - \frac{{29}}{7}{/tex}
From Eq. (i),
{tex}x\left[ {1 + 2\left( {\frac{{ - 29}}{7}} \right)} \right] + y\left( {2 - \frac{{29}}{7}} \right) + z\left( {\frac{{29}}{7} + 3} \right) - 4 + 5\left( {\frac{{ - 29}}{7}} \right) = 0{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow {/tex} x(7 - 58) + y(14 - 29) + z(29 + 21) - 28 - 145 = 0
{tex} \Rightarrow {/tex} -51x - 15y + 50z - 173 = 0
So, the required equation of plane is 51x + 15y - 50z + 173 = 0.

question_text: The plane ax + by = 0 is rotated about its line of intersection with the plane z = 0 through an angle {tex}\alpha {/tex} . Prove that the equation of the plane in its new position is {tex}ax + by \pm (\sqrt {{a^2} + {b^2}} \tan \alpha )z = 0{/tex} .
question_html: The plane ax + by = 0 is rotated about its line of intersection with the plane z = 0 through an angle {tex}\alpha {/tex}. Prove that the equation of the plane in its new position is {tex}ax + by \pm (\sqrt {{a^2} + {b^2}} \tan \alpha )z = 0{/tex}.
solution_text: Equation of the plane is ax + by = 0 ..... (i) {tex}\therefore {/tex} Equation of the plane after new position is {tex}\frac{{ax\cos \alpha }}{{\sqrt {{a^2} + {b^2}} }} + \frac{{by\cos \alpha }}{{\sqrt {{b^2} + {a^2}} }} \pm z\sin \alpha = 0{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \frac{{ax}}{{\sqrt {{a^2} + {b^2}} }} + \frac{{by}}{{\sqrt {{b^2} + {a^2}} }} \pm z\tan \alpha = 0{/tex} [on dividing by cos {tex}\alpha {/tex} ] {tex} \Rightarrow ax + by \pm z\tan \alpha \sqrt {{\alpha ^2} + {b^2}} = 0{/tex} [on multiplying with {tex}\sqrt {{a^2} + {b^2}} {/tex} ] Alternate Method Given, planes are ax + by = 0 ...... (i) And z = 0 .... (ii) Therefore, the equation of any plane passing through the line of intersection of planes (i) and (ii) may be taken as ax + by + kz = 0 ....... (iii) Then, direction cosines of a normal to the plane (iii) are {tex}\frac{a}{{\sqrt {{a^2} + {b^2} + {k^2}} }},\frac{b}{{\sqrt {{a^2} + {b^2} + {k^2}} }},\frac{c}{{\sqrt {{a^2} + {b^2} + {k^2}} }}{/tex} and direction cosines of the normal to the plane (i) are {tex}\frac{a}{{\sqrt {{a^2} + {b^2}} }},\frac{b}{{\sqrt {{a^2} + {b^2}} }},0.{/tex} Since, the angle between the planes (i) and (ii) is {tex}\alpha {/tex} , {tex}\therefore \cos \alpha = \frac{{a \cdot a + b \cdot b + k \cdot 0}}{{\sqrt {{a^2} + {b^2} + {k^2}\sqrt {{a^2} + {b^2}} } }}{/tex} {tex} = \sqrt {\frac{{{a^2} + {b^2}}}{{{a^2} + {b^2} + {k^2}}}} {/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow {k^2}{\cos ^2}\alpha = {a^2}(1 - {\cos ^2}\alpha ) + {b^2}(1 - {\cos ^2}\alpha ){/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow {k^2} = \frac{{({a^2} + {b^2}){{\sin }^2}\alpha }}{{{{\cos }^2}\alpha }}{/tex} {tex}k = \pm \sqrt {{a^2} + {b^2}} \tan \alpha {/tex} On putting this value in plane (iii), we get the equation of the plane as {tex}ax + by \pm z\sqrt {{a^2} + {b^2}} \tan \alpha = 0{/tex}
solution_html:
Equation of the plane is ax + by = 0 ..... (i)
{tex}\therefore {/tex} Equation of the plane after new position is
{tex}\frac{{ax\cos \alpha }}{{\sqrt {{a^2} + {b^2}} }} + \frac{{by\cos \alpha }}{{\sqrt {{b^2} + {a^2}} }} \pm z\sin \alpha = 0{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow \frac{{ax}}{{\sqrt {{a^2} + {b^2}} }} + \frac{{by}}{{\sqrt {{b^2} + {a^2}} }} \pm z\tan \alpha = 0{/tex}[on dividing by cos {tex}\alpha {/tex}]
{tex} \Rightarrow ax + by \pm z\tan \alpha \sqrt {{\alpha ^2} + {b^2}} = 0{/tex}[on multiplying with {tex}\sqrt {{a^2} + {b^2}} {/tex}]

Alternate Method
Given, planes are ax + by = 0 ...... (i)
And z = 0 .... (ii)
Therefore, the equation of any plane passing through the line of intersection of planes (i) and (ii) may be taken as ax + by + kz = 0 ....... (iii)
Then, direction cosines of a normal to the plane (iii) are {tex}\frac{a}{{\sqrt {{a^2} + {b^2} + {k^2}} }},\frac{b}{{\sqrt {{a^2} + {b^2} + {k^2}} }},\frac{c}{{\sqrt {{a^2} + {b^2} + {k^2}} }}{/tex} and direction cosines of the normal to the plane (i) are {tex}\frac{a}{{\sqrt {{a^2} + {b^2}} }},\frac{b}{{\sqrt {{a^2} + {b^2}} }},0.{/tex}
Since, the angle between the planes (i) and (ii) is {tex}\alpha {/tex},
{tex}\therefore \cos \alpha = \frac{{a \cdot a + b \cdot b + k \cdot 0}}{{\sqrt {{a^2} + {b^2} + {k^2}\sqrt {{a^2} + {b^2}} } }}{/tex}
{tex} = \sqrt {\frac{{{a^2} + {b^2}}}{{{a^2} + {b^2} + {k^2}}}} {/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow {k^2}{\cos ^2}\alpha = {a^2}(1 - {\cos ^2}\alpha ) + {b^2}(1 - {\cos ^2}\alpha ){/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow {k^2} = \frac{{({a^2} + {b^2}){{\sin }^2}\alpha }}{{{{\cos }^2}\alpha }}{/tex}
{tex}k = \pm \sqrt {{a^2} + {b^2}} \tan \alpha {/tex}
On putting this value in plane (iii), we get the equation of the plane as
{tex}ax + by \pm z\sqrt {{a^2} + {b^2}} \tan \alpha = 0{/tex}

question_text: Find the equation of the plane through the intersection of the planes {tex}\overrightarrow r \cdot (\hat i + 3\hat j) - 6 = 0{/tex} and {tex}\overrightarrow r \cdot (3\hat i - \hat j - 4\hat k) = 0{/tex} , whose perpendicular distance from origin is unity.
question_html: Find the equation of the plane through the intersection of the planes {tex}\overrightarrow r \cdot (\hat i + 3\hat j) - 6 = 0{/tex} and {tex}\overrightarrow r \cdot (3\hat i - \hat j - 4\hat k) = 0{/tex}, whose perpendicular distance from origin is unity.
solution_text: We have, {tex}{\overrightarrow n _1} = (\hat i + 3\hat j),{d_1} = 6{/tex} and {tex}{\overrightarrow n _2} = (3\hat i - \hat j - 4\hat k),{d_2} = 0{/tex} Using the relation, {tex}\overrightarrow r \cdot ({\overrightarrow n _1} + \lambda {\overrightarrow n _2}) = {d_1} + {d_2}\lambda {/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \overrightarrow r \cdot \left[ {(\hat i + 3\hat j) + \lambda (3\hat i - \hat j - 4\hat k)} \right] = 6 + 0 \cdot \lambda {/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \overrightarrow r \cdot \left[ {(1 + 3\lambda )\vec i + (3 - \lambda )\vec j +\vec k( - 4\lambda )} \right] = 6{/tex} .... (i) On dividing both sides by {tex}\sqrt {{{(1 + 3\lambda )}^2} + {{(3 - \lambda )}^2} + {{( - 4\lambda )}^2}} {/tex} ,we get {tex}\frac{{\overrightarrow r \cdot \left[ {(1 + 3\lambda )\hat i + (3 - \lambda )\hat j + \hat k( - 4\lambda )} \right]}}{{\sqrt {{{(1 + 3\lambda )}^2} + {{(3 - \lambda )}^2} + {{( - 4\lambda )}^2}} }} = \frac{6}{{\sqrt {{{(1 + 3\lambda )}^2} + {{(3 - \lambda )}^2} + {{( - 4\lambda )}^2}} }}{/tex} Since, the perpendicular distance from origin is unity. {tex}\therefore \frac{6}{{\sqrt {{{(1 - 3\lambda )}^2} + {{(3 - \lambda )}^2} + {{( - 4\lambda )}^2}} }} = 1{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow {(1 - 3\lambda )^2} + {(3 - \lambda )^2} + {( - 4\lambda )^2} = 36{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow 1 + 9{\lambda ^2} + 6\lambda + 9 + {\lambda ^2} - 16\lambda + 16{\lambda ^2} = 36{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow 26{\lambda ^2} + 10 = 36{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow {\lambda ^2} = 1{/tex} {tex}\therefore \lambda = \pm 1{/tex} Using Eq. (i), the required equation of plane is {tex}\overrightarrow r \cdot \left[ {(1 \pm 3)\hat i + (3 \mp 1)\hat j + ( \mp 4)\hat k} \right] = 6{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \overrightarrow r \cdot \left[ {(1 + 3)\hat i + (3 - 1)\hat j + ( - 4)\hat k} \right] = 6{/tex} and {tex}\overrightarrow r \cdot \left[ {(1 - 3)\hat i + (3 + 1)\hat j + 4\hat k} \right] = 6{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \overrightarrow r \cdot (4\hat i + 2\hat j - 4\hat k) = 6{/tex} And {tex}\overrightarrow r \cdot ( - 2\hat i + 4\hat j + 4\hat k) = 6{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow 4x + 2y - 4z - 6 = 0{/tex} and -2x + 4y + 4z - 6 = 0
solution_html:
We have, {tex}{\overrightarrow n _1} = (\hat i + 3\hat j),{d_1} = 6{/tex} and {tex}{\overrightarrow n _2} = (3\hat i - \hat j - 4\hat k),{d_2} = 0{/tex}
Using the relation, {tex}\overrightarrow r \cdot ({\overrightarrow n _1} + \lambda {\overrightarrow n _2}) = {d_1} + {d_2}\lambda {/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow \overrightarrow r \cdot \left[ {(\hat i + 3\hat j) + \lambda (3\hat i - \hat j - 4\hat k)} \right] = 6 + 0 \cdot \lambda {/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow \overrightarrow r \cdot \left[ {(1 + 3\lambda )\vec i + (3 - \lambda )\vec j +\vec k( - 4\lambda )} \right] = 6{/tex}.... (i)
On dividing both sides by {tex}\sqrt {{{(1 + 3\lambda )}^2} + {{(3 - \lambda )}^2} + {{( - 4\lambda )}^2}} {/tex} ,we get
{tex}\frac{{\overrightarrow r \cdot \left[ {(1 + 3\lambda )\hat i + (3 - \lambda )\hat j + \hat k( - 4\lambda )} \right]}}{{\sqrt {{{(1 + 3\lambda )}^2} + {{(3 - \lambda )}^2} + {{( - 4\lambda )}^2}} }} = \frac{6}{{\sqrt {{{(1 + 3\lambda )}^2} + {{(3 - \lambda )}^2} + {{( - 4\lambda )}^2}} }}{/tex}
Since, the perpendicular distance from origin is unity.
{tex}\therefore \frac{6}{{\sqrt {{{(1 - 3\lambda )}^2} + {{(3 - \lambda )}^2} + {{( - 4\lambda )}^2}} }} = 1{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow {(1 - 3\lambda )^2} + {(3 - \lambda )^2} + {( - 4\lambda )^2} = 36{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow 1 + 9{\lambda ^2} + 6\lambda + 9 + {\lambda ^2} - 16\lambda + 16{\lambda ^2} = 36{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow 26{\lambda ^2} + 10 = 36{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow {\lambda ^2} = 1{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \lambda = \pm 1{/tex}
Using Eq. (i), the required equation of plane is
{tex}\overrightarrow r \cdot \left[ {(1 \pm 3)\hat i + (3 \mp 1)\hat j + ( \mp 4)\hat k} \right] = 6{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow \overrightarrow r \cdot \left[ {(1 + 3)\hat i + (3 - 1)\hat j + ( - 4)\hat k} \right] = 6{/tex}
and {tex}\overrightarrow r \cdot \left[ {(1 - 3)\hat i + (3 + 1)\hat j + 4\hat k} \right] = 6{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow \overrightarrow r \cdot (4\hat i + 2\hat j - 4\hat k) = 6{/tex}
And {tex}\overrightarrow r \cdot ( - 2\hat i + 4\hat j + 4\hat k) = 6{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow 4x + 2y - 4z - 6 = 0{/tex}
and -2x + 4y + 4z - 6 = 0

question_text: Show that the points {tex}(\hat i - \hat j + 3\hat k){/tex} and {tex}3(\hat i + \hat j + \hat k){/tex} are equidistant from the plane {tex}\overrightarrow r \cdot (5\hat i + 2\hat j - 7\hat k) + 9 = 0{/tex} and lies on the opposite side of it.
question_html: Show that the points {tex}(\hat i - \hat j + 3\hat k){/tex} and {tex}3(\hat i + \hat j + \hat k){/tex} are equidistant  from the plane {tex}\overrightarrow r \cdot (5\hat i + 2\hat j - 7\hat k) + 9 = 0{/tex} and lies on the opposite side of it.
solution_text: To show that these given points {tex}(\hat i - \hat j + 3\hat k){/tex} and {tex}3(\hat i + \hat j + \hat k){/tex} are equidistant from the plane {tex}\overrightarrow r \cdot (5\hat i + 2\hat j - 7\hat k) + 9 = 0{/tex} , we first find out the mid-point of the points which is {tex}=\frac{(\vec i-\vec j+3\vec k)+3(\vec i+\vec j+\vec k)}{2}{/tex} = {tex}2\hat i + \hat j + 3\hat k{/tex} On substituting {tex}\overrightarrow r {/tex} by the mid-point in plane, we get {tex}LHS = (2\vec i +\vec j + 3\vec k) \cdot (5\hat i + 2\hat j - 7\hat k) + 9{/tex} = 10 + 2 - 21 + 9 = 0 = RHS Hence, the two points lie on opposite sides of the plane are equidistant from the plane.
solution_html:
To show that these given points {tex}(\hat i - \hat j + 3\hat k){/tex} and {tex}3(\hat i + \hat j + \hat k){/tex} are equidistant from the plane
{tex}\overrightarrow r \cdot (5\hat i + 2\hat j - 7\hat k) + 9 = 0{/tex}, we first find out the mid-point of the points which is{tex}=\frac{(\vec i-\vec j+3\vec k)+3(\vec i+\vec j+\vec k)}{2}{/tex} ={tex}2\hat i + \hat j + 3\hat k{/tex}
On substituting {tex}\overrightarrow r {/tex} by the mid-point in plane, we get
{tex}LHS = (2\vec i +\vec j + 3\vec k) \cdot (5\hat i + 2\hat j - 7\hat k) + 9{/tex}
= 10 + 2 - 21 + 9 = 0
= RHS
Hence, the two points lie on opposite sides of the plane are equidistant from the plane.

question_text: {tex}\overrightarrow {AB} = 3\hat i - \hat j + \hat k{/tex} and {tex}\overrightarrow {CD} = - 3\hat i + 2\hat j + 4\hat k{/tex} are two vectors. The position vectors of the points A and C are {tex}6\hat i + 7\hat j + 4\hat k{/tex} and {tex} - 9\hat j + 2\hat k{/tex} , respectively. Find the position vector of a point P on the line AB and a point Q on the line CD such that {tex}\overrightarrow {PQ} {/tex} is perpendicular to {tex}\overrightarrow {AB} {/tex} and {tex}\overrightarrow {CD} {/tex} both.
question_html: {tex}\overrightarrow {AB} = 3\hat i - \hat j + \hat k{/tex} and {tex}\overrightarrow {CD} = - 3\hat i + 2\hat j + 4\hat k{/tex} are two vectors. The position vectors of the points A and C are {tex}6\hat i + 7\hat j + 4\hat k{/tex} and {tex} - 9\hat j + 2\hat k{/tex}, respectively. Find the position vector of a point P on the line AB and a point Q on the line CD such that {tex}\overrightarrow {PQ} {/tex} is perpendicular to {tex}\overrightarrow {AB} {/tex} and {tex}\overrightarrow {CD} {/tex} both.
solution_text: We have, {tex}\overrightarrow {AB} = 3\hat i - \hat j + \hat k{/tex} and {tex}\overrightarrow {CD} = - 3\hat i + 2\hat j + 4\hat k{/tex} Also, the position vectors of A and C are {tex}6\hat i + 7\hat j + 4\hat k{/tex} and {tex} - 9\hat j + 2\hat k{/tex} , respectively. Since, {tex}\overrightarrow {PQ} {/tex} is perpendicular to both {tex}\overrightarrow {AB} {/tex} and {tex}\overrightarrow {CD} {/tex} . So, P and Q will be foot of perpendicular to both the lines through A and C. Now, equation of the line through A and parallel to the vector {tex}\overrightarrow {AB} {/tex} is, {tex}\overrightarrow r = (6\hat i + 7\hat j + 4\hat k) + \lambda (3\hat i - \hat j + \hat k){/tex} And the line through C and parallel to the vector {tex}\overrightarrow {CD} {/tex} is given by {tex}\overrightarrow r = - 9\hat j + 2\hat k + \mu ( - 3\hat i + 2\hat j + 4\hat k){/tex} ..... (i) Let {tex}\overrightarrow r = (6i + 7j + 4\hat k) + \lambda (3\hat i - \hat j + \hat k){/tex} and {tex}\overrightarrow r = - 9\hat j + 2\hat k + \mu ( - 3\hat i + 2\hat j + 4\hat k){/tex} .... (ii) Let {tex}P(6 + 3\lambda ,7 - \lambda ,4 + \lambda ){/tex} is any point on the first line and Q be any point on second line is given by {tex}\left( { - 3\mu , - 9 + 2\mu ,2 + 4\mu } \right){/tex} . {tex}\therefore \overrightarrow {PQ} = ( - 3\mu - 6 - 3\lambda )\hat i + ( - 9 + 2\mu - 7 + \lambda )\hat j + (2 + 4\mu - 4 - \lambda )\hat k{/tex} {tex} = ( - 3\mu - 6 - 3\lambda )\hat i + (2\mu + \lambda - 16)\hat j + (4\mu - \lambda - 2)\hat k{/tex} If {tex}\overrightarrow {PQ} {/tex} is perpendicular to the first line, then {tex}3( - 3\mu - 6 - 3\lambda ) - (2\mu + \lambda - 16) + (4\mu - \lambda - 2) = 0{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow - 9\mu - 18 - 9\lambda - 2\mu - \lambda + 16 + 4\mu - \lambda - 2 = 0{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow - 7\mu - 11\lambda - 4 = 0{/tex} ..... (iii) If {tex}\overrightarrow {PQ} {/tex} is perpendicular to the second line, then {tex} - 3( - 3\mu - 6 - 3\lambda ) + (2\mu + \lambda - 16) + (4\mu - \lambda + 2) = 0{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow 9\mu + 18 + 9\lambda + 4\mu + 2\lambda - 32 + 16\mu - 4\lambda - 8 = 0{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow 29\mu + 7\lambda - 22 = 0{/tex} ..... (iv) On solving Eqs. (iii) and (iv), we get {tex} - 49\mu - 77\lambda - 28 = 0{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow 319\mu + 77\lambda - 242 = 0{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow 270\mu - 270 = 0{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \mu = 1{/tex} Using {tex}\mu {/tex} in Eq. (iii), we get {tex} - 7(1) = - 11\lambda - 4 = 0{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow - 7 - 11\lambda - 4 = 0{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow - 11 - 11\lambda = 0{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \lambda = - 1{/tex} {tex}\therefore \overrightarrow {PQ} = \left[ { - 3(1) - 6 - 3( - 1)} \right]\hat i + \left[ {2(1) + ( - 1) - 16} \right]\hat j + \left[ {4(1) - ( - 1) - 2} \right]\hat k{/tex} {tex} = - 6\hat i - 15\hat j + 3\hat k{/tex}
solution_html:
We have, {tex}\overrightarrow {AB} = 3\hat i - \hat j + \hat k{/tex} and {tex}\overrightarrow {CD} = - 3\hat i + 2\hat j + 4\hat k{/tex}
Also, the position vectors of A and C are {tex}6\hat i + 7\hat j + 4\hat k{/tex} and {tex} - 9\hat j + 2\hat k{/tex}, respectively. Since, {tex}\overrightarrow {PQ} {/tex} is perpendicular to both {tex}\overrightarrow {AB} {/tex} and {tex}\overrightarrow {CD} {/tex}.
So, P and Q will be foot of perpendicular to both the lines through A and C.
Now, equation of the line through A and parallel to the vector {tex}\overrightarrow {AB} {/tex} is,
{tex}\overrightarrow r = (6\hat i + 7\hat j + 4\hat k) + \lambda (3\hat i - \hat j + \hat k){/tex}
And the line through C and parallel to the vector {tex}\overrightarrow {CD} {/tex} is given by
{tex}\overrightarrow r = - 9\hat j + 2\hat k + \mu ( - 3\hat i + 2\hat j + 4\hat k){/tex} ..... (i)
Let {tex}\overrightarrow r = (6i + 7j + 4\hat k) + \lambda (3\hat i - \hat j + \hat k){/tex}
and {tex}\overrightarrow r = - 9\hat j + 2\hat k + \mu ( - 3\hat i + 2\hat j + 4\hat k){/tex} .... (ii)
Let {tex}P(6 + 3\lambda ,7 - \lambda ,4 + \lambda ){/tex} is any point on the first line and Q be any point on second line is given by {tex}\left( { - 3\mu , - 9 + 2\mu ,2 + 4\mu } \right){/tex}.
{tex}\therefore \overrightarrow {PQ} = ( - 3\mu - 6 - 3\lambda )\hat i + ( - 9 + 2\mu - 7 + \lambda )\hat j + (2 + 4\mu - 4 - \lambda )\hat k{/tex}
{tex} = ( - 3\mu - 6 - 3\lambda )\hat i + (2\mu + \lambda - 16)\hat j + (4\mu - \lambda - 2)\hat k{/tex}
If {tex}\overrightarrow {PQ} {/tex} is perpendicular to the first line, then
{tex}3( - 3\mu - 6 - 3\lambda ) - (2\mu + \lambda - 16) + (4\mu - \lambda - 2) = 0{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow - 9\mu - 18 - 9\lambda - 2\mu - \lambda + 16 + 4\mu - \lambda - 2 = 0{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow - 7\mu - 11\lambda - 4 = 0{/tex} ..... (iii)
If {tex}\overrightarrow {PQ} {/tex} is perpendicular to the second line, then
{tex} - 3( - 3\mu - 6 - 3\lambda ) + (2\mu + \lambda - 16) + (4\mu - \lambda + 2) = 0{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow 9\mu + 18 + 9\lambda + 4\mu + 2\lambda - 32 + 16\mu - 4\lambda - 8 = 0{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow 29\mu + 7\lambda - 22 = 0{/tex} ..... (iv)
On solving Eqs. (iii) and (iv), we get
{tex} - 49\mu - 77\lambda - 28 = 0{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow 319\mu + 77\lambda - 242 = 0{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow 270\mu - 270 = 0{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow \mu = 1{/tex}
Using {tex}\mu {/tex} in Eq. (iii), we get
{tex} - 7(1) = - 11\lambda - 4 = 0{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow - 7 - 11\lambda - 4 = 0{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow - 11 - 11\lambda = 0{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow \lambda = - 1{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \overrightarrow {PQ} = \left[ { - 3(1) - 6 - 3( - 1)} \right]\hat i + \left[ {2(1) + ( - 1) - 16} \right]\hat j + \left[ {4(1) - ( - 1) - 2} \right]\hat k{/tex}
{tex} = - 6\hat i - 15\hat j + 3\hat k{/tex}

question_text: Show that the straight lines whose direction cosines are given by 2l + 2m - n = 0 and mn + nl + lm = 0 are at right angles.
question_html: Show that the straight lines whose direction cosines are given by 2l + 2m - n = 0 and mn + nl + lm = 0 are at right angles.
solution_text: We have, 2l + 2m - n = 0 .... (i) And mn + nl + lm = 0 .... (ii) Eliminating m from the both equations, we get {tex}m = \frac{{n - 2l}}{2}{/tex} [from Eq. (i)] {tex} \Rightarrow \left( {\frac{{n - 2l}}{2}} \right)n + nl + l\left( {\frac{{n - 2l}}{2}} \right) = 0{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \frac{{{n^2} - 2nl + 2nl + nl - 2{l^2}}}{2} = 0{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow {/tex} n 2 + nl - 2l 2 = 0 {tex} \Rightarrow {/tex} n 2 + 2nl - nl - 2l 2 = 0 {tex} \Rightarrow {/tex} (n + 2l)(n - l) = 0 {tex} \Rightarrow {/tex} n = -2l and n = l {tex}\therefore m = \frac{{ - 2l - 2l}}{2},m = \frac{{l - 2l}}{2}{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow m = - 2l,m = \frac{{ - l}}{2}{/tex} Thus, the direction ratios of two lines are proportional to l, -2l, -2 and {tex}l,\frac{{ - l}}{2},l{/tex} . {tex} \Rightarrow {/tex} 1, -2, -2 and {tex}1,\frac{{ - 1}}{2},1{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow {/tex} 1, -2, -2 and 2, -1, 2 Also, the vectors parallel to these lines are {tex}\overrightarrow a = \hat i - 2\hat j + 2\hat k{/tex} and {tex}\overrightarrow b = 2\hat i - \hat j + 2\hat k{/tex} respectively. {tex}\therefore \cos \theta = \frac{{\overrightarrow a \cdot \overrightarrow b }}{{\left| {\overrightarrow a } \right|\left| {\overrightarrow b } \right|}} = \frac{{(i - 2\hat j - 2\hat k) \cdot (2\hat i - \hat j + 2\hat k)}}{{3 \cdot 3}}{/tex} {tex} = \frac{{2 + 2 - 4}}{9} = 0{/tex} {tex}\therefore \theta = \frac{\pi }{2}\left[ {\because \cos \frac{\pi }{2} = 0} \right]{/tex}
solution_html:
We have, 2l + 2m - n = 0 .... (i)
And mn + nl + lm = 0 .... (ii)
Eliminating m from the both equations, we get
{tex}m = \frac{{n - 2l}}{2}{/tex} [from Eq. (i)]
{tex} \Rightarrow \left( {\frac{{n - 2l}}{2}} \right)n + nl + l\left( {\frac{{n - 2l}}{2}} \right) = 0{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow \frac{{{n^2} - 2nl + 2nl + nl - 2{l^2}}}{2} = 0{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow {/tex} n2 + nl - 2l2 = 0
{tex} \Rightarrow {/tex} n2 + 2nl - nl - 2l2 = 0
{tex} \Rightarrow {/tex} (n + 2l)(n - l) = 0
{tex} \Rightarrow {/tex} n = -2l and n = l
{tex}\therefore m = \frac{{ - 2l - 2l}}{2},m = \frac{{l - 2l}}{2}{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow m = - 2l,m = \frac{{ - l}}{2}{/tex}
Thus, the direction ratios of two lines are proportional to l, -2l, -2 and {tex}l,\frac{{ - l}}{2},l{/tex}.
{tex} \Rightarrow {/tex} 1, -2, -2 and {tex}1,\frac{{ - 1}}{2},1{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow {/tex} 1, -2, -2 and 2, -1, 2
Also, the vectors parallel to these lines are {tex}\overrightarrow a = \hat i - 2\hat j + 2\hat k{/tex} and {tex}\overrightarrow b = 2\hat i - \hat j + 2\hat k{/tex} respectively.
{tex}\therefore \cos \theta = \frac{{\overrightarrow a \cdot \overrightarrow b }}{{\left| {\overrightarrow a } \right|\left| {\overrightarrow b } \right|}} = \frac{{(i - 2\hat j - 2\hat k) \cdot (2\hat i - \hat j + 2\hat k)}}{{3 \cdot 3}}{/tex}
{tex} = \frac{{2 + 2 - 4}}{9} = 0{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \theta = \frac{\pi }{2}\left[ {\because \cos \frac{\pi }{2} = 0} \right]{/tex}

question_text: If l 1 , m 1 , n 1 ; l 2 , m 2 , n 2 ; l 3 , m 3 , n 3 are the direction cosines of three mutually perpendicular lines, prove that the line whose direction cosines are proportional to l 1 + l 2 + l 3 , m 1 + m 2 + m 3 , n 1 + n 2 + n 3 makes equal angles with them.
question_html: If l1, m1, n1; l2, m2, n2; l3, m3, n3 are the direction cosines of three mutually perpendicular lines, prove that the line whose direction cosines are proportional to l1 + l2 + l3, m1 + m2 + m3, n1 + n2 + n3 makes equal angles with them.
solution_text: Let {tex}\overrightarrow a = {l_1}\hat i + {m_1}\hat j + {n_1}\hat k{/tex} {tex}\overrightarrow b = {l_2}\hat i + {m_2}\hat j + {n_2}\hat k{/tex} {tex}\overrightarrow c = {l_3}\hat i + {m_3}\hat j + {n_3}\hat k{/tex} {tex}\overrightarrow d = ({l_1} + {l_2} + {l_3})\hat i + ({m_1} + {m_2} + {m_3})\hat j + ({n_1} + {n_2} + {n_3})\hat k{/tex} Also, let {tex}\alpha ,\beta {/tex} and {tex}\gamma {/tex} are the angles between {tex}\overrightarrow a {/tex} and {tex}\overrightarrow d ,\overrightarrow b {/tex} and {tex}\overrightarrow d ,\overrightarrow c {/tex} and {tex}\overrightarrow d {/tex} . {tex}\therefore \cos \alpha = {l_1}({l_1} + {l_2} + {l_3}) + {m_1}({m_1} + {m_2} + {m_3}) + {n_1}({n_1} + {n_2} + {n_3}){/tex} {tex} = l_1^2 + {l_1}{l_2} + {l_1}{l_3} + m_1^2 + {m_1}{m_2} + {m_1}{m_3} + n_1^3 + {n_1}{n_2} + {n_1}{n_3}{/tex} {tex} = \left( {l_1^2 + m_1^2 + n_1^2} \right) + \left( {{l_1}{l_2} + {l_1}{l_3} + {m_1}{m_2} + {m_1}{m_3} + {n_1}{n_2} + {n_1}{n_3}} \right){/tex} = 1 + 0 = 1 {tex}[\because l_1^2 + m_1^2 + n_1^2 = 1{/tex} and {tex}{l_1} \bot {l_2},{l_1} \bot {l_3},{m_1} \bot {m_2},{m_1} \bot {m_3},{n_1} \bot {n_2},{n_1} \bot {n_3}]{/tex} Similarly, {tex}\cos \beta = {l_2}({l_1} + {l_2} + {l_3}) + {m_2}({m_1} + {m_2} + {m_3}) + {n_2}({n_1} + {n_2} + {n_3}){/tex} = 1 + 0=1 and {tex}\cos \gamma = 1 + 0{/tex} =0 {tex} \Rightarrow \cos \alpha = \cos \beta = \cos \gamma {/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \alpha = \beta = \gamma {/tex} So, the line whose direction cosines are proportional to l 1 + l 2 + l 2 , m 1 + m 2 + m 3 , n 1 + n 2 + n 3 make equal to angles with the three mutually perpendicular lines whose direction cosines are l 1 , m 1 , n 1 , l 2 , m 2 , n 2 and l 3 , m 3 , n 3 respectively.
solution_html:
Let
{tex}\overrightarrow a = {l_1}\hat i + {m_1}\hat j + {n_1}\hat k{/tex}
{tex}\overrightarrow b = {l_2}\hat i + {m_2}\hat j + {n_2}\hat k{/tex}
{tex}\overrightarrow c = {l_3}\hat i + {m_3}\hat j + {n_3}\hat k{/tex}
{tex}\overrightarrow d = ({l_1} + {l_2} + {l_3})\hat i + ({m_1} + {m_2} + {m_3})\hat j + ({n_1} + {n_2} + {n_3})\hat k{/tex}
Also, let {tex}\alpha ,\beta {/tex} and {tex}\gamma {/tex} are the angles between {tex}\overrightarrow a {/tex} and {tex}\overrightarrow d ,\overrightarrow b {/tex} and {tex}\overrightarrow d ,\overrightarrow c {/tex} and {tex}\overrightarrow d {/tex}.
{tex}\therefore \cos \alpha = {l_1}({l_1} + {l_2} + {l_3}) + {m_1}({m_1} + {m_2} + {m_3}) + {n_1}({n_1} + {n_2} + {n_3}){/tex}
{tex} = l_1^2 + {l_1}{l_2} + {l_1}{l_3} + m_1^2 + {m_1}{m_2} + {m_1}{m_3} + n_1^3 + {n_1}{n_2} + {n_1}{n_3}{/tex}
{tex} = \left( {l_1^2 + m_1^2 + n_1^2} \right) + \left( {{l_1}{l_2} + {l_1}{l_3} + {m_1}{m_2} + {m_1}{m_3} + {n_1}{n_2} + {n_1}{n_3}} \right){/tex}
= 1 + 0 = 1
{tex}[\because l_1^2 + m_1^2 + n_1^2 = 1{/tex} and {tex}{l_1} \bot {l_2},{l_1} \bot {l_3},{m_1} \bot {m_2},{m_1} \bot {m_3},{n_1} \bot {n_2},{n_1} \bot {n_3}]{/tex}
Similarly, {tex}\cos \beta = {l_2}({l_1} + {l_2} + {l_3}) + {m_2}({m_1} + {m_2} + {m_3}) + {n_2}({n_1} + {n_2} + {n_3}){/tex}
= 1 + 0=1 and {tex}\cos \gamma = 1 + 0{/tex}=0
{tex} \Rightarrow \cos \alpha = \cos \beta = \cos \gamma {/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow \alpha = \beta = \gamma {/tex}
So, the line whose direction cosines are proportional to l1 + l2 + l2, m1 + m2 + m3, n1 + n2 + n3 make equal to angles with the three mutually perpendicular lines whose direction cosines are l1, m1, n1, l2, m2, n2 and l3, m3, n3 respectively.

question_html: The sine of the angle between the straight line {tex}\frac{x-2}{3}=\frac{y-3}{4}=\frac{z-4}{5}{/tex} and the plane 2x - 2y + z = 5 is

options.explanation: We have, the equation of line as {tex}\frac{x-2}{3}=\frac{y-3}{4}=\frac{z-4}{5}{/tex} This line is parallel to the vector {tex}\vec{b}=3 \hat{i}+4 \hat{j}+5 \hat{k}{/tex} Equation of plane is 2x - 2y + z = 5 Normal to the plane is {tex}\vec{n}=2 \hat{i}-2 \hat{j}+\hat{k}{/tex} Its angle between line and plane is {tex}\theta{/tex} . Then, {tex}\sin \theta=\frac{|\vec{b} \cdot \vec{n}|}{|\vec{b}||\vec{n}|}=\frac{|(3 \hat{i}+4 \hat{j}+5 \hat{k}) \cdot(2 \hat{i}-2 \hat{j}+\hat{k})|}{\sqrt{3^{2}+4^{2}+5^{2}} \sqrt{4+4+1}}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{|6-8+5|}{\sqrt{50} \sqrt{9}}=\frac{3}{15 \sqrt{2}}=\frac{1}{5 \sqrt{2}}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\sin \theta=\frac{\sqrt{2}}{10}{/tex}
