question_html: The area of the quadrilateral ABCD, where A(0,4,1), B (2, 3, -1), C(4, 5, 0) and D (2, 6, 2), is equal to

options.explanation: We have, A(0,4,1), B (2, 3, -1), C(4, 5, 0) and D (2, 6, 2) {tex}\therefore \overrightarrow{A B}=(2-0) \hat{i}+(3-4) \hat{j}+(-1-1) \hat{k}=2 \hat{i}-\hat{j}-2 \hat{k}{/tex} {tex}\overrightarrow {B C}=(4-2) \hat{i}+(5-3) \hat{j}+(0+1) \hat{k}=2 \hat{i}+2 \hat{j}+\hat{k}{/tex} {tex}\overrightarrow{C D}=(2-4) \hat{i}+(6-5) \hat{j}+(2-0) \hat{k}=-2 \hat{i}+\hat{j}+2 \hat{k}{/tex} {tex}\overrightarrow{D A}=(0-2) \hat{i}+(4-6) \hat{i}+(1-2) \hat{k}=-2 \hat{i}-2 \hat{j}-\hat{k}{/tex} Thus quadrilateral formed is parallelogram. {tex}\therefore{/tex} Area of quadrilateral ABCD {tex}=|\overrightarrow{A B} \times \overrightarrow{B C}|=\left|\begin{array}{ccc} {i} & {j} & {\hat{k}} \\ {2} & {-1} & {-2} \\ {2} & {2} & {1} \end{array}\right|{/tex} {tex}=|3 \hat{i}-6 \hat{j}+6 \hat{k}|{/tex} {tex}=\sqrt{9+36+36}{/tex} = 9 sq. units

question_html: If the direction ratios of a line are 1,1,2 find the direction cosines of the line.
solution_text: The direction cosines are given by {tex}l = \frac{a}{{\sqrt {a^2+ {b^2} + c^2} }},m = \frac{b}{{\sqrt {{a^2} + {b^2} + {c^2}} }},n = \frac{c}{{\sqrt {{a^2} + {b^2} + {c^2}} }}{/tex} Here a,b,c are 1,1,2 respectively. Therefore, {tex}l = \frac{1}{{\sqrt {{1^2} + {1^2} + {2^2}} }},m = \frac{1}{{\sqrt {{1^2} + {1^2} + {2^2}} }},n = \frac{2}{{\sqrt {{1^2} + {1^2} + {2^2}} }}{/tex} i.e., {tex}l = \frac{1}{{\sqrt 6 }},m = \frac{1}{{\sqrt 6 }},n = \frac{2}{{\sqrt 6 }}{/tex} i.e. {tex} \left( {\frac{1}{{\sqrt 6 }},\frac{1}{{\sqrt 6 }},\frac{2}{{\sqrt 6 }}} \right){/tex} are D.C’s of the line.
solution_html:
The direction cosines are given by
{tex}l = \frac{a}{{\sqrt {a^2+ {b^2} + c^2} }},m = \frac{b}{{\sqrt {{a^2} + {b^2} + {c^2}} }},n = \frac{c}{{\sqrt {{a^2} + {b^2} + {c^2}} }}{/tex}
Here a,b,c are 1,1,2 respectively.
Therefore, {tex}l = \frac{1}{{\sqrt {{1^2} + {1^2} + {2^2}} }},m = \frac{1}{{\sqrt {{1^2} + {1^2} + {2^2}} }},n = \frac{2}{{\sqrt {{1^2} + {1^2} + {2^2}} }}{/tex}
i.e., {tex}l = \frac{1}{{\sqrt 6 }},m = \frac{1}{{\sqrt 6 }},n = \frac{2}{{\sqrt 6 }}{/tex} i.e. {tex} \left( {\frac{1}{{\sqrt 6 }},\frac{1}{{\sqrt 6 }},\frac{2}{{\sqrt 6 }}} \right){/tex}are D.C’s of the line.

question_text: Find the direction cosines of the line passing through the points P(2, 3, 5) and Q(-1, 2, 4).
question_html: Find the direction cosines of the line passing through the points P(2, 3, 5) and Q(-1, 2, 4).
solution_text: We know that the direction cosines of a line passing through the points P(x 1 , y 1 , z 1 ) and Q(x 2 , y 2 , z 2 ) are {tex}\frac{{{x_2} - {x_1}}}{{PQ}},\frac{{{y_2} - {y_1}}}{{PQ}},\frac{{{z_2} - {z_1}}}{{PQ}}{/tex} . Here {tex}PQ = \sqrt {{{\left( {{x_2} - {x_1}} \right)}^2} + \left( {{y_2} - {y_1}} \right)^2 + {{\left( {{z_2} - {z_1}} \right)}^2}} {/tex} {tex}= \sqrt {{{\left( { - 1 - 2} \right)}^2} + {{\left( {2 - 3} \right)}^2} + {{\left( {4 - 5} \right)}^2}} = \sqrt {9 + 1 + 1} = \sqrt {11} {/tex} Hence Direction cosines of given line are {tex}\pm \left( {\frac{{ - 3}}{{\sqrt {11} }}\frac{{ - 1}}{{\sqrt {11} }},\frac{{ - 1}}{{\sqrt {11} }}} \right){/tex} {tex}{/tex}
solution_html:
We know that the direction cosines of a line passing through the points P(x1, y1, z1) and Q(x2, y2, z2) are
{tex}\frac{{{x_2} - {x_1}}}{{PQ}},\frac{{{y_2} - {y_1}}}{{PQ}},\frac{{{z_2} - {z_1}}}{{PQ}}{/tex}.
Here {tex}PQ = \sqrt {{{\left( {{x_2} - {x_1}} \right)}^2} + \left( {{y_2} - {y_1}} \right)^2 + {{\left( {{z_2} - {z_1}} \right)}^2}} {/tex}
{tex}= \sqrt {{{\left( { - 1 - 2} \right)}^2} + {{\left( {2 - 3} \right)}^2} + {{\left( {4 - 5} \right)}^2}} = \sqrt {9 + 1 + 1} = \sqrt {11} {/tex}
Hence Direction cosines of given line are
{tex}\pm \left( {\frac{{ - 3}}{{\sqrt {11} }}\frac{{ - 1}}{{\sqrt {11} }},\frac{{ - 1}}{{\sqrt {11} }}} \right){/tex}  {tex}{/tex}

question_text: The x - coordinate of a point on the line joining the points Q(2, 2, 1) and R(5, 1, -2) is 4. Find its z - coordinate.
question_html: The x - coordinate of a point on the line joining the points Q(2, 2, 1) and R(5, 1, -2) is 4. Find its z - coordinate.
solution_text: Let the point P divide QR in the ratio {tex}\lambda :1{/tex} , then the co-ordinate of P are {tex}\left( {\frac{{5\lambda + 2}}{{\lambda + 1}},\frac{{\lambda + 2}}{{\lambda + 1}},\frac{{ - 2\lambda + 1}}{{\lambda + 1}}} \right){/tex} But x - coordinate of P is 4. Therefore, {tex}\frac{{5\lambda + 2}}{{\lambda + 1}} = 4 \Rightarrow \lambda = 2{/tex} Hence, the z - coordinate of P is {tex}\frac{{ - 2\lambda + 1}}{{\lambda + 1}} = - 1{/tex} .
solution_html:
Let the point P divide QR in the ratio {tex}\lambda :1{/tex}, then the co-ordinate of P are

{tex}\left( {\frac{{5\lambda + 2}}{{\lambda + 1}},\frac{{\lambda + 2}}{{\lambda + 1}},\frac{{ - 2\lambda + 1}}{{\lambda + 1}}} \right){/tex}

But x - coordinate of P is 4. Therefore,

{tex}\frac{{5\lambda + 2}}{{\lambda + 1}} = 4 \Rightarrow \lambda = 2{/tex}

Hence, the z - coordinate of P is {tex}\frac{{ - 2\lambda + 1}}{{\lambda + 1}} = - 1{/tex}.

question_html: Find the distance of the point whose position vector is {tex}(2 i+j-\tilde{k}){/tex} from the plane {tex}\vec{r} \cdot(\hat{i}-2 \hat{j}+4 \hat{k})=9{/tex}
solution_text: Here {tex}\vec{a}=2 \hat{i}+\hat{j}-\hat{k}, \vec{n}=\hat{i}-2 \hat{j}+4 \hat{k}{/tex} and d = 9 So, the required distance is {tex}\frac{|(2 \hat{i}+\hat{j}-\hat{k}) \cdot(\hat{i}-2 \hat{j}+4 \hat{k})-9|}{\sqrt{1+4+16}}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{|2-2-4-9|}{\sqrt{21}}=\frac{13}{\sqrt{21}}{/tex}

question_text: Find the distance of the point (-2, 4, -5) from the line {tex}\frac{x+3}{3}=\frac{y-4}{5}=\frac{z+8}{6}{/tex}
question_html: Find the distance of the point (-2, 4, -5) from the line {tex}\frac{x+3}{3}=\frac{y-4}{5}=\frac{z+8}{6}{/tex}
solution_text: Here P (-2, 4, -5) is the given point. Any point Q on the line is given by {tex}(3 \lambda-3,5 \lambda+4,6 \lambda-8){/tex} , {tex}\vec{\mathrm{PQ}}=(3 \lambda-1) \hat{i}+5 \lambda \hat{j}+(6 \lambda-3) \hat{k}{/tex} . Since {tex}\vec{\mathrm{PQ}} \perp(3 \hat{i}+5 \hat{j}+6 \hat{k}){/tex} , we have {tex}3(3 \lambda-1)+5(5 \lambda)+6(6 \lambda-3)=0{/tex} {tex}9 \lambda+25 \lambda+36 \lambda=21, \text { i.e. } \lambda=\frac{3}{10}{/tex} Thus {tex}\overrightarrow{\mathrm{PQ}}=-\frac{1}{10} \hat{i}+\frac{15}{10} \hat{j}-\frac{12}{10} \hat{k}{/tex} Hence {tex}|\overrightarrow{\mathrm{PQ}}|=\frac{1}{10} \sqrt{1+225+144}=\sqrt{\frac{37}{10}}{/tex}
solution_html:
Here P (-2, 4, -5) is the given point. Any point Q on the line is given by {tex}(3 \lambda-3,5 \lambda+4,6 \lambda-8){/tex},
{tex}\vec{\mathrm{PQ}}=(3 \lambda-1) \hat{i}+5 \lambda \hat{j}+(6 \lambda-3) \hat{k}{/tex}.
Since {tex}\vec{\mathrm{PQ}} \perp(3 \hat{i}+5 \hat{j}+6 \hat{k}){/tex}, we have
{tex}3(3 \lambda-1)+5(5 \lambda)+6(6 \lambda-3)=0{/tex}
{tex}9 \lambda+25 \lambda+36 \lambda=21, \text { i.e. } \lambda=\frac{3}{10}{/tex}
Thus {tex}\overrightarrow{\mathrm{PQ}}=-\frac{1}{10} \hat{i}+\frac{15}{10} \hat{j}-\frac{12}{10} \hat{k}{/tex}
Hence {tex}|\overrightarrow{\mathrm{PQ}}|=\frac{1}{10} \sqrt{1+225+144}=\sqrt{\frac{37}{10}}{/tex}

question_text: Find the coordinates of the point where the line through (3, -4, -5) and (2, -3, 1) crosses the plane passing through three points (2, 2, 1), (3, 0, 1) and (4, -1, 0)
question_html: Find the coordinates of the point where the line through (3, -4, -5) and (2, -3, 1) crosses the plane passing through three points (2, 2, 1), (3, 0, 1) and (4, -1, 0)
solution_text: Equation of plane through three points (2, 2, 1), (3, 0, 1) and (4, -1, 0) is {tex}[(\vec{r}-(2 \hat{i}+2 \hat{j}+\hat{k})] \cdot[(\hat{i}-2 \hat{j}) \times(\hat{i}-\hat{j}-\hat{k})]=0{/tex} i.e. {tex}\vec{r} \cdot(2 \hat{i}+\hat{j}+\hat{k})=7{/tex} or 2x + y + z - 7 = 0 ...(i) Equation of line through (3, -4, -5) and (2, -3, 1) is {tex}\frac{x-3}{-1}=\frac{y+4}{1}=\frac{z+5}{6}{/tex} ....(ii) Any point on line (ii) is {tex}(-\lambda+3, \lambda-4,6 \lambda-5){/tex} . This point lies on plane (i). Therefore, {tex}2(-\lambda+3)+(\lambda-4)+(6 \lambda-5)-7=0{/tex} , i.e., {tex}\lambda=z{/tex} Hence the required point is (1, -2, 7).
solution_html:
Equation of plane through three points (2, 2, 1), (3, 0, 1) and (4, -1, 0) is {tex}[(\vec{r}-(2 \hat{i}+2 \hat{j}+\hat{k})] \cdot[(\hat{i}-2 \hat{j}) \times(\hat{i}-\hat{j}-\hat{k})]=0{/tex}
i.e. {tex}\vec{r} \cdot(2 \hat{i}+\hat{j}+\hat{k})=7{/tex} or 2x + y + z - 7 = 0 ...(i)
Equation of line through (3, -4, -5) and (2, -3, 1) is
{tex}\frac{x-3}{-1}=\frac{y+4}{1}=\frac{z+5}{6}{/tex} ....(ii)
Any point on line (ii) is {tex}(-\lambda+3, \lambda-4,6 \lambda-5){/tex}.
This point lies on plane (i).
Therefore, {tex}2(-\lambda+3)+(\lambda-4)+(6 \lambda-5)-7=0{/tex},
i.e., {tex}\lambda=z{/tex}
Hence the required point is (1, -2, 7).

question_text: Find the distance of the point (-1,-5,-10) from the point of intersection of the line {tex}\overrightarrow r = (2\hat i - \hat j + 2\hat k) + \lambda (3\hat i + 4\hat j + 2\hat k){/tex} and the plane {tex}\overrightarrow r \cdot \left( {\hat i - \hat j + \hat k} \right) = 5{/tex} .
question_html: Find the distance of the point (-1,-5,-10) from the point of intersection of the line {tex}\overrightarrow r = (2\hat i - \hat j + 2\hat k) + \lambda (3\hat i + 4\hat j + 2\hat k){/tex} and the plane {tex}\overrightarrow r \cdot \left( {\hat i - \hat j + \hat k} \right) = 5{/tex}.
solution_text: {tex}\vec r = \left( {2i - j + 2k} \right) + \lambda \left( {3i + 4j + 2k} \right){/tex} Then, in Cartesian form,we have {tex}\frac{{x - 2}}{3} = \frac{{y + 1}}{4} = \frac{{z - 2}}{2} = \lambda {/tex} ..... (i) coordinates of any point on (i) is , {tex}3\lambda + 2,4\lambda - 1,2\lambda + 2{/tex} The equation of plane is {tex}\overrightarrow r . \left( {\hat i - \hat j + \hat k} \right) = 5{/tex} {tex}\left( {x\hat i + y\hat j + z\hat k} \right) \cdot \left( {\hat i - \hat j + \hat k} \right) = 5{/tex} x - y + z = 5 .... (ii) If the point {tex}(3\lambda+2,4\lambda-1,2\lambda+2){/tex} lies on (ii),then {tex}(3\lambda+2)-(4\lambda-1)+(2\lambda+2)=5{/tex} {tex}\lambda+5=5{/tex} {tex}\lambda = 0{/tex} we get (2, -1, 2) as the coordinate of the point of intersection of the given line and the plane. Now distance between the points (-1, -5, -10) and (2, -1, 2) {tex} = \sqrt {{{(2 + 1)}^2} + {{( - 1 - 5)}^2} + {{(2 + 10)}^2}} {/tex} = 13

{tex}\vec r = \left( {2i - j + 2k} \right) + \lambda \left( {3i + 4j + 2k} \right){/tex}

Then, in Cartesian form,we have
{tex}\frac{{x - 2}}{3} = \frac{{y + 1}}{4} = \frac{{z - 2}}{2} = \lambda {/tex} ..... (i)
coordinates of any point on (i) is ,
{tex}3\lambda + 2,4\lambda - 1,2\lambda + 2{/tex}
The equation of plane is

 {tex}\overrightarrow r . \left( {\hat i - \hat j + \hat k} \right) = 5{/tex}
{tex}\left( {x\hat i + y\hat j + z\hat k} \right) \cdot \left( {\hat i - \hat j + \hat k} \right) = 5{/tex}
x - y + z = 5 .... (ii)

If the point {tex}(3\lambda+2,4\lambda-1,2\lambda+2){/tex}lies on (ii),then

question_text: A plane meets the co-ordinates axis in A, B, C such that the centroid of the {tex}\triangle{/tex} ABC is the point {tex}(\alpha, \beta, \gamma){/tex} . Show that the equation of the plane is {tex}\frac{x}{\alpha}+\frac{y}{\beta}+\frac{z}{\gamma}=3{/tex}
question_html: A plane meets the co-ordinates axis in A, B, C such that the centroid of the {tex}\triangle{/tex}ABC is the point {tex}(\alpha, \beta, \gamma){/tex}. Show that the equation of the plane is {tex}\frac{x}{\alpha}+\frac{y}{\beta}+\frac{z}{\gamma}=3{/tex}
solution_text: Let the equation of the plane be {tex}\frac{x}{a}+\frac{y}{b}+\frac{z}{c}=1{/tex} Then the co-ordinate of A, B, C are (a, 0, 0), (0,b,0) and (0, 0, c) respectively. Centroid of the {tex}\triangle{/tex} ABC is {tex}\left(\frac{x_{1}+x_{2}+x_{3}}{3}, \frac{y_{1}+y_{2}+y_{3}}{3}, \frac{z_{1}+z_{2}+z_{3}}{3}\right){/tex} i.e. {tex}\left(\frac{a}{3}, \frac{b}{3}, \frac{c}{3}\right){/tex} But co-ordinates of the centroid of the {tex}\triangle{/tex} ABC are {tex}(\alpha, \beta, \gamma){/tex} (given). Therefore, {tex}\alpha=\frac{a}{3}, \beta=\frac{b}{3}, \gamma=\frac{c}{3}{/tex} , i.e., {tex}a=3 \alpha, b=3 \beta, c=3 \gamma{/tex} Thus, the equation of plane is {tex}\frac{x}{\alpha}+\frac{y}{\beta}+\frac{z}{\gamma}=3{/tex}
solution_html:
Let the equation of the plane be
{tex}\frac{x}{a}+\frac{y}{b}+\frac{z}{c}=1{/tex}
Then the co-ordinate of A, B, C are (a, 0, 0), (0,b,0) and (0, 0, c) respectively.
Centroid of the {tex}\triangle{/tex}ABC is {tex}\left(\frac{x_{1}+x_{2}+x_{3}}{3}, \frac{y_{1}+y_{2}+y_{3}}{3}, \frac{z_{1}+z_{2}+z_{3}}{3}\right){/tex} i.e. {tex}\left(\frac{a}{3}, \frac{b}{3}, \frac{c}{3}\right){/tex}
But co-ordinates of the centroid of the {tex}\triangle{/tex}ABC are {tex}(\alpha, \beta, \gamma){/tex} (given).
Therefore, {tex}\alpha=\frac{a}{3}, \beta=\frac{b}{3}, \gamma=\frac{c}{3}{/tex}, i.e., {tex}a=3 \alpha, b=3 \beta, c=3 \gamma{/tex}
Thus, the equation of plane is
{tex}\frac{x}{\alpha}+\frac{y}{\beta}+\frac{z}{\gamma}=3{/tex}

question_text: Find the angle between the lines whose direction cosines are given by the equations: 3l + m + 5n = 0 and 6mn - 2nl + 5lm = 0.
question_html: Find the angle between the lines whose direction cosines are given by the equations: 3l + m + 5n = 0 and 6mn - 2nl + 5lm = 0.
solution_text: Given equations are; 3l + m + 5n = 0 and 6mn - 2nl + 5lm = 0 Eliminating m from the above two equations, we get {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 2n 2 + 3ln + l 2 = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (n + l)(2n + l) = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} either n = -l or l = -2n Now if l = -n, then m = -2n and if l = -2n, then m = n. Thus the direction ratios of two lines are proportional to -n, -2n, n and -2n, n, n, i.e. 1, 2, -1 and -2, 1, 1. So, vectors parallel to these lines are {tex}\vec{a}=\hat{i}+2 \hat{j}-\hat{k}{/tex} and {tex}\vec{b}=-2 \hat{i}+\hat{j}+\hat{k}{/tex} , respectively. If {tex}\theta{/tex} is the angle between the lines, then {tex}\cos \theta=\frac{\vec{a} \cdot \vec{b}}{|\vec{a}||\vec{b}|}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{(\hat{i}+2 \hat{j}-\hat{k}) \cdot(-2 \hat{i}+\hat{j}+\hat{k})}{\sqrt{1^{2}+2^{2}+(-1)^{2}} \sqrt{(-2)^{2}+1^{2}+1^{2}}}=-\frac{1}{6}{/tex} Hence {tex}\theta=\cos ^{-1}\left(-\frac{1}{6}\right){/tex}
solution_html:
Given equations are; 
3l + m + 5n = 0 and
6mn - 2nl + 5lm = 0
Eliminating m from the above two equations, we get
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 2n2 + 3ln + l2 = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (n + l)(2n + l) = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} either n = -l or l = -2n
Now if l = -n, then m = -2n
and if l = -2n, then m = n.
Thus the direction ratios of two lines are proportional to -n, -2n, n and -2n, n, n,
i.e. 1, 2, -1 and -2, 1, 1.
So, vectors parallel to these lines are
{tex}\vec{a}=\hat{i}+2 \hat{j}-\hat{k}{/tex} and {tex}\vec{b}=-2 \hat{i}+\hat{j}+\hat{k}{/tex}, respectively.
If {tex}\theta{/tex} is the angle between the lines, then
{tex}\cos \theta=\frac{\vec{a} \cdot \vec{b}}{|\vec{a}||\vec{b}|}{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{(\hat{i}+2 \hat{j}-\hat{k}) \cdot(-2 \hat{i}+\hat{j}+\hat{k})}{\sqrt{1^{2}+2^{2}+(-1)^{2}} \sqrt{(-2)^{2}+1^{2}+1^{2}}}=-\frac{1}{6}{/tex}
Hence {tex}\theta=\cos ^{-1}\left(-\frac{1}{6}\right){/tex}

question_text: Find the co-ordinates of the foot of perpendicular drawn from the point A (1, 8, 4) to the line joining the points B (0, –1, 3) and C (2, –3, –1).
question_html: Find the co-ordinates of the foot of perpendicular drawn from the point A (1, 8, 4) to the line joining the points B (0, –1, 3) and C (2, –3, –1).
solution_text: Let L be the foot of perpendicular drawn from the points A (1, 8, 4) to the line passing through B and C as shown in the Figure. The equation of line BC by using formula {tex}\vec{r}=\vec{a}+\lambda(\vec{b}-\vec{a}){/tex} , the equation of the line BC is {tex}\vec{r}=(-\hat{j}+3 \hat{k})+\lambda(2 \hat{i}-2 \hat{j}-4 \hat{k}){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad x \hat{i}+y \hat{i}+z \hat{k}=2 \lambda \hat{i}-(2 \lambda+1) \hat{i}+\lambda(3-4 \lambda) \hat{k}{/tex} Comparing both sides, we get {tex}x=2 \lambda, y=-(2 \lambda+1), z=3-4 \lambda{/tex} ...(i) Thus, the co-ordinate of L are {tex}(2 \lambda,-(2 \lambda+1),(3-4 \lambda){/tex} , so that the direction ratios of the line AL are {tex}(1-2 \lambda), 8+(2 \lambda+1), 4-(3-4 \lambda){/tex} , i.e. {tex}1-2 \lambda, 2 \lambda+9,1+4 \lambda{/tex} Since AL is perpendicular BC, we have, {tex}-(1-2 \lambda)(2-0)+(2 \lambda+9)(-3+1)+(4 \lambda+1)(-1-3)=0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \lambda=\frac{-5}{6}{/tex} The required point is obtained by substituting the value of {tex}\lambda{/tex} , in (i), which is {tex}\left(\frac{-5}{3}, \frac{2}{3}, \frac{19}{3}\right){/tex} .
solution_html:
Let L be the foot of perpendicular drawn from the points A (1, 8, 4) to the line passing through B and C as shown in the Figure. The equation of line BC by using formula {tex}\vec{r}=\vec{a}+\lambda(\vec{b}-\vec{a}){/tex}, the equation of the line BC is
{tex}\vec{r}=(-\hat{j}+3 \hat{k})+\lambda(2 \hat{i}-2 \hat{j}-4 \hat{k}){/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad x \hat{i}+y \hat{i}+z \hat{k}=2 \lambda \hat{i}-(2 \lambda+1) \hat{i}+\lambda(3-4 \lambda) \hat{k}{/tex}
Comparing both sides, we get
{tex}x=2 \lambda, y=-(2 \lambda+1), z=3-4 \lambda{/tex} ...(i)
Thus, the co-ordinate of L are {tex}(2 \lambda,-(2 \lambda+1),(3-4 \lambda){/tex},
so that the direction ratios of the line AL are {tex}(1-2 \lambda), 8+(2 \lambda+1), 4-(3-4 \lambda){/tex}, i.e.
{tex}1-2 \lambda, 2 \lambda+9,1+4 \lambda{/tex}
Since AL is perpendicular BC, we have,
{tex}-(1-2 \lambda)(2-0)+(2 \lambda+9)(-3+1)+(4 \lambda+1)(-1-3)=0{/tex}

{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \lambda=\frac{-5}{6}{/tex}
The required point is obtained by substituting the value of {tex}\lambda{/tex}, in (i), which is {tex}\left(\frac{-5}{3}, \frac{2}{3}, \frac{19}{3}\right){/tex}.

question_text: Find the image of the point (1, 6, 3) in the line {tex}\frac{x}{1} = \frac{{y - 1}}{2} = \frac{{z - 2}}{3}{/tex} .
question_html: Find the image of the point (1, 6, 3) in the line {tex}\frac{x}{1} = \frac{{y - 1}}{2} = \frac{{z - 2}}{3}{/tex}.
solution_text: Let {tex}P\left( {1,6,3} \right){/tex} be the given point and let L be the foot of perpendicular from P to the given line. The coordinates of a general point on the given line are {tex}\frac{{x - 1}}{1} = \frac{{y - 1}}{2} = \frac{{z - 2}}{3} = \lambda {/tex} i.e., {tex}x = \lambda ,y = 2\lambda + 1,z = 3\lambda + 2{/tex} If the coordinates of L are {tex}\left( {\lambda ,2\lambda + 1,3\lambda + 2} \right){/tex} , then the direction ratios of PL are {tex}\lambda - 1,2\lambda - 5,3\lambda - 1{/tex} . But the direction ratios of given line which is perpendicular to PL are 1,2,3. Therefore, {tex}\left( {\lambda - 1} \right) + \left( {2\lambda - 5} \right)2 + \left( {3\lambda - 1} \right)3 = 0{/tex} which gives {tex}\lambda = 1{/tex} . Hence coordinate of L are (1, 3, 5). Let {tex}Q\left( {{x_1},{y_1},{z_1}} \right){/tex} be the image of {tex}P\left( {1,6,3} \right){/tex} in the given line. Then L is the mid-point of PQ. Therefore, {tex}\frac{{{x_1} + 1}}{2} = 1,\frac{{{y_1} + 6}}{2} = 3,\frac{{{z_1} + 3}}{2} = 5{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow {x_1} = 1,{y_1} = 0,{z_1} = 7{/tex} Hence, the image of (1, 6, 3) in the given line is (1, 0, 7).
solution_html:
Let {tex}P\left( {1,6,3} \right){/tex}be the given point and let L be the foot of perpendicular from P to the given line.

The coordinates of a general point on the given line are

{tex}\frac{{x - 1}}{1} = \frac{{y - 1}}{2} = \frac{{z - 2}}{3} = \lambda {/tex} i.e., {tex}x = \lambda ,y = 2\lambda + 1,z = 3\lambda + 2{/tex}

If the coordinates of L are {tex}\left( {\lambda ,2\lambda + 1,3\lambda + 2} \right){/tex}, then the direction ratios of PL are {tex}\lambda - 1,2\lambda - 5,3\lambda - 1{/tex}.
But the direction ratios of given line which is perpendicular to PL are 1,2,3. Therefore, {tex}\left( {\lambda - 1} \right) + \left( {2\lambda - 5} \right)2 + \left( {3\lambda - 1} \right)3 = 0{/tex}which gives {tex}\lambda = 1{/tex}. Hence coordinate of L are (1, 3, 5).
Let {tex}Q\left( {{x_1},{y_1},{z_1}} \right){/tex}be the image of {tex}P\left( {1,6,3} \right){/tex} in the given line. Then L is the mid-point of PQ. Therefore, {tex}\frac{{{x_1} + 1}}{2} = 1,\frac{{{y_1} + 6}}{2} = 3,\frac{{{z_1} + 3}}{2} = 5{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow {x_1} = 1,{y_1} = 0,{z_1} = 7{/tex}
Hence, the image of (1, 6, 3) in the given line is (1, 0, 7).

question_text: Find the image of the point having position vector {tex}\hat i + 3\hat j + 4\hat k{/tex} in the plane {tex}r.\left( {2\hat i - \hat j + \hat k} \right) + 3 = 0{/tex}
question_html: Find the image of the point having position vector {tex}\hat i + 3\hat j + 4\hat k{/tex} in the plane {tex}r.\left( {2\hat i - \hat j + \hat k} \right) + 3 = 0{/tex}
solution_text: Let the given point be {tex}P\left( {\hat i + 3\hat j + 4\hat k} \right){/tex} and Q be the image of P in the plane {tex}\hat r.\left( {2\hat i - \hat j + \hat k} \right) + 3 = 0{/tex} as shown in the Fig. Then PQ is the normal to the plane. Since PQ passes through P and is normal to the given plane, so the equation of PQ is given by {tex}\vec r = \left( {\hat i + 3\hat j + 4\hat k} \right) + \lambda \left( {2\hat i - \hat j + \hat k} \right){/tex} Since Q lies on the line PQ, the position vector of Q can be expressed as {tex}\left( {\hat i + 3\hat j + 4\hat k} \right) + \lambda \left( {2\hat i - \hat j + \hat k} \right){/tex} i.e., {tex}\left( {1 + 2\lambda } \right)i + \left( {3 - \lambda } \right)\hat j + 4\left( {4 + \lambda } \right)\hat k{/tex} Since R is the mid point of PQ, the position vector of R is {tex}\frac{{\left[ {\left( {1 + 2\lambda } \right)\hat i + \left( {3 - \lambda } \right)\hat j + \left( {4 + \lambda } \right)\hat k} \right] + \left[ {\hat i + 3\hat j + 4\hat k} \right]}}{2}{/tex} i.e., {tex}\left( {\lambda + 1} \right)\hat i + \left( {3 - \frac{\lambda }{2}} \right)\hat j + \left( {4 + \frac{\lambda }{2}} \right)\hat k{/tex} Again, since R lies on the plane {tex}\vec r.\left( {2\hat i - \hat j + \hat k} \right) + 3 = 0{/tex} , we have {tex}\left\{ {\left( {\lambda - 1} \right)\hat i + \left( {3 - \frac{\lambda }{2}} \right)\hat j + \left( {4 + \frac{\lambda }{2}} \right)\hat k} \right\}.\left( {2\hat i - \hat j - \hat k} \right) + 3 = 0{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \lambda = - 2{/tex} Hence, the position vector of Q is {tex}\left( {\hat i + 3\hat j + 4\hat k} \right) - 2\left( {2\hat i - \hat j + \hat k} \right){/tex} , i.e., {tex}- 3\hat i + 5\hat j + 2\hat k{/tex} .
solution_html:
Let the given point be {tex}P\left( {\hat i + 3\hat j + 4\hat k} \right){/tex} and Q be the image of P in the plane {tex}\hat r.\left( {2\hat i - \hat j + \hat k} \right) + 3 = 0{/tex}as shown in the Fig.

Then PQ is the normal to the plane. Since PQ passes through P and is normal to the given plane, so the equation of PQ is given by

{tex}\vec r = \left( {\hat i + 3\hat j + 4\hat k} \right) + \lambda \left( {2\hat i - \hat j + \hat k} \right){/tex}

Since Q lies on the line PQ, the position vector of Q can be expressed as {tex}\left( {\hat i + 3\hat j + 4\hat k} \right) + \lambda \left( {2\hat i - \hat j + \hat k} \right){/tex} i.e., {tex}\left( {1 + 2\lambda } \right)i + \left( {3 - \lambda } \right)\hat j + 4\left( {4 + \lambda } \right)\hat k{/tex}

Since R is the mid point of PQ, the position vector of R is {tex}\frac{{\left[ {\left( {1 + 2\lambda } \right)\hat i + \left( {3 - \lambda } \right)\hat j + \left( {4 + \lambda } \right)\hat k} \right] + \left[ {\hat i + 3\hat j + 4\hat k} \right]}}{2}{/tex}

i.e., {tex}\left( {\lambda + 1} \right)\hat i + \left( {3 - \frac{\lambda }{2}} \right)\hat j + \left( {4 + \frac{\lambda }{2}} \right)\hat k{/tex}

Again, since R lies on the plane {tex}\vec r.\left( {2\hat i - \hat j + \hat k} \right) + 3 = 0{/tex}, we have

{tex}\left\{ {\left( {\lambda - 1} \right)\hat i + \left( {3 - \frac{\lambda }{2}} \right)\hat j + \left( {4 + \frac{\lambda }{2}} \right)\hat k} \right\}.\left( {2\hat i - \hat j - \hat k} \right) + 3 = 0{/tex}

{tex} \Rightarrow \lambda = - 2{/tex}

Hence, the position vector of Q is {tex}\left( {\hat i + 3\hat j + 4\hat k} \right) - 2\left( {2\hat i - \hat j + \hat k} \right){/tex}, i.e., {tex}- 3\hat i + 5\hat j + 2\hat k{/tex}.

solution_text: Note that sin 2 {tex}\alpha{/tex} + sin 2 {tex}\beta{/tex} + sin 2 {tex}\gamma{/tex} = (1 - cos 2 {tex}\alpha{/tex} ) + (1 - cos 2 {tex}\beta{/tex} ) + (1 - cos 2 {tex}\gamma{/tex} ) = 3 - (cos 2 {tex}\alpha{/tex} + cos 2 {tex}\beta{/tex} + cos 2 {tex}\gamma{/tex} ) = 2.
